General safety and regulatory information
Important safety instructions
1.1 Important safety instructions
It is important to install, operate, and do the maintenance correctly on all medical electrical
equipment. This is most important for safety related items. For your safety and the safety of the
patients, Elekta recommends that you read, understand, and obey all:
• Warnings, cautions, and notes in this document and related documents
• Warnings, cautions, and safety markings on the equipment and the accessories of the
equipment
• Safety instructions and information in this document and related documents.
Before you operate, or do work on the equipment, it is important that you read and understand
the emergency procedures.
1.1.1 Conventions for warnings, cautions, and notes
WARNING 1.1
A warning is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions, where the result can be fatal
or serious injury, injury, or clinical mistreatment, if you do not obey the instructions.
CAUTION 1.1
A caution is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions, where the result can be
damage to the equipment, data, or the environment, if you do not obey the instructions.
Note: A note gives more information about a related text.
Precise Treatment Table™ Document ID: 45133701862 10

Compliance of the equipment with international standards
1.2 Compliance of the equipment with international standards

If necessary, contact Elekta Limited for more information on the applicable safety standards.
1.2.2 IEC classification of the product
WARNING 1.2
Do not use this equipment unless the electrical power supply is connected to a grounded earth
to prevent an electrical shock. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.

1.3 Recommendations for training
Different countries have different regulations for training. Make sure that you have the necessary
training before you operate or do work on the equipment. Make sure that your training is in
compliance with the laws and regulations of the jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed.
Training is available from Elekta. Contact Elekta for more information about the applicable
training courses.
Document ID: 45133701862 10 Precise Treatment Table™
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 10 ©2019 Elekta Limited.

General safety and regulatory information
Recommendations for equipment change of ownership
1.4 Recommendations for equipment change of ownership
If you get the equipment from a previous owner, Elekta recommends that you contact the local
Elekta representative and:
• Tell them about the new ownership of the equipment.
• Tell them about the location of the equipment.
• Get copies of the applicable documentation (there is a cost for this service).
• Get information on the different types of service Elekta can give to customers.
1.5 Recommendations for emergency procedures
Before you use the equipment, Elekta recommends that you know all the local emergency
procedures and the locations of all emergency switches, such as the:
• STOP MOTORS buttons on the handheld controllers and the control panels in the
treatment room
• EMERGENCY OFF switches in the treatment room, the control area, and in areas near to
the equipment.
1.6 Safety precautions
Elekta recommends that:
• All users obey the precautions in this section.
• You examine the warnings, cautions and notes in this document and use them in your
local Work Instructions.
1.6.1 Safety precautions for the compatibility of the equipment
WARNING 1.3
Do not use parts or accessories that are not approved by Elekta. The result can be incorrect
radiation delivery and other safety risks. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury
or clinical mistreatment.
Only operate the equipment with Elekta supplied or approved, compatible equipment or parts.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of other equipment or parts.
Do not use accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by Elekta. They can have an
effect on the performance of the electromagnetic compatibility (EMC), which can increase the
emissions or decrease the immunity of the equipment.
1.6.2 Safety precautions for work with heavy parts
WARNING 1.4
Do not lift or move heavy parts unless you obey the applicable Health and Safety regulations
and procedures. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
Precise Treatment Table™ Document ID: 45133701862 10
Corrective Maintenance Manual
©2019 Elekta Limited. Page 11

General safety and regulatory information
Safety precautions
1.6.3 Safety precautions for work with visual display units
WARNING 1.5
Do not use a visual display unit (VDU) unless you obey the applicable Health and Safety
regulations and procedures. If you ignore this warning, it can cause injury.
1.6.4 Safety precautions for operation of the equipment
WARNING 1.6
Do not use the equipment if you know or think it is defective or incorrectly calibrated. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, and damage to the
equipment.
Before you operate the equipment, make sure that:
• There are no defective parts.
• The equipment calibration is correct.
• The user routine checks are completed correctly.
• The planned maintenance schedule is completed correctly.
Refer to the applicable section of the applicable Instructions for Use for information on the user
routine checks and the planned maintenance schedule.
1.6.5 Safety precautions for work with safety devices during clinical
operation
WARNING 1.7
Do not remove, change, or override the switches, interlocks, and other safety devices. The
result can be incorrect radiation delivery and other safety risks. If you ignore this warning, it
can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
1.6.6 Safety precautions for work with safety devices during service
operation
WARNING 1.8
Do not remove, change, or override the switches, interlocks, or other safety devices unless
special instructions in this document tell you to. The result can be incorrect radiation delivery
and other safety risks. When the work is completed, set all the safety devices again and do a
test of them. If you ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
Document ID: 45133701862 10 Precise Treatment Table™
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 12 ©2019 Elekta Limited.

General safety and regulatory information
Safety precautions
1.6.7 Safety precautions for work with electrical and mechanical parts
WARNING 1.9
Do not start to do work on the linear accelerator before you isolate the power to the work area
at the interface cabinet. You can get a high voltage electric shock. If you ignore this warning, it
can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.10
Do not remove the covers or cables from the equipment unless special instructions in this
document tell you to. The covers give access to high voltage electric parts. Always install the
covers and cables again before you use the equipment. If you ignore this warning, it can cause
fatal injury.
WARNING 1.11
Do not remove the covers from the equipment unless special instructions in this document tell
you to. The covers give access to parts that move. Always install the covers again before you
use the equipment. If you ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.12
Be careful when the power supply switch of the equipment is put in the off position. There
continues to be a risk of high voltage electric shock. Some of the customer interfaces can
continue to supply electrical power to the electrical terminals. If you ignore this warning, it can
cause fatal injury.
Only operate the equipment in rooms that are in compliance with the applicable electrical safety
laws and regulations for this type of equipment.
Before you start the maintenance and repair work, or start to clean the equipment, isolate the
electrical power supply and prevent unwanted movement of the equipment.
1.6.8 Safety precautions for work with devices that are sensitive to an
electrostatic discharge
CAUTION 1.2
Do not touch the PCBs and other electronic parts unless you wear a grounded antistatic wrist
strap. The result can be an electrostatic discharge and a malfunction. If you ignore this caution,
you can cause damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 1.3
Do not touch the parts identified with the ESD caution label. Always obey the ESD safety
precautions when you do work on these parts. If you ignore this caution, you can cause
damage to the equipment.
Semiconductor devices, integrated circuits, and the parts that contain them, are sensitive to ESD.
These devices are known as electrostatic discharge sensitive devices. ESD can cause damage to
these devices and cause them to have a malfunction immediately or some time after the
electrostatic discharge. To prevent the ESD damage to these devices, always wear a grounded
antistatic wrist strap, and obey all the ESD precautions and procedures.
Precise Treatment Table™ Document ID: 45133701862 10
Corrective Maintenance Manual
©2019 Elekta Limited. Page 13

General safety and regulatory information
Safety precautions
Elekta recommends that all users receive the training about ESD. The training must include the
ESD caution label and these ESD safety precautions.
1.6.9 Safety precautions for the occurrence of fire or explosion
WARNING 1.13
Do not use the equipment in rooms that contain explosive or flammable gases. High levels of
oxygen, and some anesthetic gases and disinfectant sprays, are explosive or flammable. If you
ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
1.6.10 Safety precautions for the electromagnetic compatibility
WARNING 1.14
Do not put portable or mobile radio frequency (RF) communication devices, or other electrical
devices, near the equipment. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical
mistreatment, and damage to the equipment.
The Elekta products are in compliance with applicable EMC emission standards. It is possible that
the electromagnetic emissions from the portable and mobile RF communication devices, or other
electrical devices, are more than is permitted by the EMC standards. Such electromagnetic
emissions can have an unwanted effect on the operation of the medical electrical equipment.
WARNING 1.15
Do not use the accessories, transducers, and cables unless they are specified by Elekta. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury and clinical mistreatment.
Special precautions for EMC emissions are necessary. See the related documentation on how to
do the installation and setting to work procedures, and to operate the equipment.
See the section Technical Description in the Instructions for Use for information about EMC.
1.6.11 Safety precautions for cleaning and disinfection
WARNING 1.16
Do not clean the equipment, or use a disinfectant spray, before you isolate the equipment
from the electrical supply. You can get a high voltage electric shock. If you ignore this warning,
it can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.17
Do not use a spray that is explosive or flammable. The fumes from such sprays can cause an
explosion or start a fire. If you ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury and damage to the
equipment.
CAUTION 1.4
Do not let water and other liquids go into the equipment. The results can be electrical short
circuits and metal corrosion. If you ignore this caution, it can cause damage to the equipment.
Document ID: 45133701862 10 Precise Treatment Table™
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 14 ©2019 Elekta Limited.

General safety and regulatory information
Safety precautions
CAUTION 1.5
Do not use a spray to clean the medical equipment room. The spray can go into the equipment.
The results can be electrical short circuits and metal corrosion. If you ignore this caution, it can
cause damage to the equipment.
At regular intervals, it is necessary to clean the equipment and equipment room with
disinfectants. This section gives the information on the general procedures. Where special
procedures and precautions are necessary, the related documents give the information on these
procedures. The procedures for the disinfection and cleaning of the equipment and the
equipment room must obey the applicable laws and regulations of the jurisdiction.
1.6.11.1 Recommended general cleaning procedure
Clean surfaces with a mild detergent solution. Then, dry the surface with a clean, lint-free cloth.
Do not use a solution, solvent, abrasive detergent, or polish that is corrosive. If you do not know
the properties of the solution, do not use it.
1.6.11.2 Recommended cleaning procedure for patient surfaces
Elekta recommends that you use one of these procedures to clean the surfaces that can touch
the patient during treatment:
• Use a clean, lint-free cloth and apply, by hand, a detergent solution made from water
and a non-corrosive biocide. (Do not use a biocide made from chlorine bleach).
• Use a clean, lint-free cloth to apply, by hand, denatured alcohol (ethanol) to the
surfaces.
1.7 Disposal of the equipment
WARNING 1.18
Do not move or discard radioactive and hazardous material unless you are a qualified person
and have an applicable license. Obey the laws and regulations of the jurisdiction for disposal
and movement of these materials. If you ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury and
damage to the environment.
End of life (EOL) disposal refers to the procedure to remove and discard equipment, or parts of
the equipment, to a condition where it will not be possible to operate the equipment for its
intended use.
Always use procedures that give the best possible protection to the environment when you
remove and discard the equipment. The procedures must obey the laws and regulations of the
jurisdictions in which you discard the equipment. Do not discard Elekta equipment through the
domestic waste systems.
Only an approved facility with an applicable license must remove and discard the equipment and
recycle the material. Do not discard material that is hazardous to health and the environment
together with other material.
Before you remove and discard equipment, contact Elekta for information.

Introduction
Document scope
2.1 Document scope
The Precise Treatment Table™ operates with the Elekta Medical Linear Accelerator range of
products and systems:
• Precise Digital Linear Accelerator (Precise Treatment System™)
• Electa Synergy® Platform
• Elekta Synergy®
• Elekta Infinity™
• Versa HD™
• Axesse™ (for China only)
The corrective maintenance manual (CMM) helps service engineers troubleshoot, repair and
maintain the device's systems, subsystems and components. The target audience is qualified
service engineers. These may be engineers employed directly by Elekta, qualified representatives
of Elekta or qualified representatives of Elekta's customers.
The manual provides:
• A description of the system or sub-system in question
• A troubleshooting section to identify the problem areas
• A set of detailed procedures used to replace FRUs (field replaceable units)
• A section on how to return the device to work.
If you find any mistakes in this document, or identify areas for improvement, please raise a CLM
(Customer Lifecycle Management) case and assign it to the relevant queue.
2.2 Expected service life of the equipment
WARNING 2.1
Do not operate the equipment unless you correctly complete the safety checks, planned
maintenance schedules, and extended life checks. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
The expected service life of this equipment is 15 years of normal use.
The Elekta definition for 'expected service life' is the length of time that the regular service and
repair procedures can keep the equipment safe to operate.
The Elekta definition of 'normal use' is the treatment of 50 patients each day for five days each
week of the year.
The level of operation of the equipment can be different to ‘normal use’. This has an effect on
the length of the service life.
Because of wear of parts, the risk of a malfunction to occur can increase after 10 years operation.
Elekta recommends that before you continue operation after 10 years, you complete the regular
extended life checks.
Elekta can help the user with the correct safety checks, planned maintenance schedules, and the
regular extended life checks for continued safe operation of the equipment.
For information on the above checks and schedules, contact your local Elekta representative.

Product description
Primary function and general configuration of the treatment table
3.1 Primary function and general configuration of the
treatment table
The treatment table is part of an Elekta digital accelerator installation. The primary function of
the treatment table is to support the patient and move them accurately to the correct position
for treatment.
There are two areas that divide the general configuration of the treatment table into its primary
parts:
• Area 800, the treatment table assembly
• Area 801, the electrical interface module.

3.2 Description of the treatment table movements
3.2.1 Ranges of movement
The treatment table has five ranges of movement:
• Longitudinal (X-axis)
• Lateral (Y-axis)
• Vertical (Z-axis)
• Isocentric floor rotation (I-axis)
• Support column rotation (C-axis).

3.2.2 Conventions for the longitudinal, lateral, and vertical ranges of
movement
Unless told differently, the conventions for the ranges of movement apply when:
• The gantry of the host machine is at the 0° position
• The Gun end (G-end) end of the treatment table top points to the gantry
• The treatment table aligns with the G-T axis of the treatment room.

Figure 3.7 Conventions for longitudinal, lateral, and vertical (height) ranges of movement
(1) T-direction longitudinal movement (5) G-direction longitudinal movement
(2) A-direction lateral movement (6) B-direction lateral movement
(3) Height movement (7) Height movement
(4) Machine isocenter
Longitudinal movement is along the horizontal axis in the G (gun) and T (target) directions. A
maximum of 1000 mm of movement is possible. Movement to the gantry of the host machine is
a positive longitudinal movement. Movement away from the gantry is a negative longitudinal
movement.
Lateral movement is along the horizontal axis in the A and B direction. A maximum of ±250 mm
of movement from the isocenter, in each direction, is possible. Movement in the B direction is a
positive lateral movement. Movement in the A direction is a negative lateral movement.
Height movement is along the vertical (Z) axis. A maximum of 1100 mm of movement is possible
from the lowest position to the highest position. Movement up is a negative height movement.
Movement down is a positive height movement. The distance from the isocenter will be different
for different types of treatment table tops.
Note: When there is electrical power supply to the treatment table, the UPS can give an audible sound
when you move the treatment table up. It does not occur when you move the treatment table
down. The audible sound is not an indication of a system fault.

3.2.3 Conventions for the iso-rotation ranges of movement
Isocentric rotation is movement around the I-axis. A maximum of 100° of rotation from the 0°
position is possible in each direction.
The conventions for isocentric rotation ranges of movement are different for the different IEC
standards. In Service mode and the IEC 60601 standard, clockwise movement is positive
isocentric rotation. Counterclockwise movement is negative isocentric rotation.
In the IEC 61217 standard, clockwise movement is negative isocentric rotation. Counterclockwise
movement iis positive isocentric rotation. The digital accelerator has the IEC 61217 configuration
set in the factory. You can change the configuration on site.

3.2.4 Conventions for the column rotation ranges of movement
The column rotation movement is around the C axis. A maximum of 180° of rotation from the 0°
position is possible in each direction. Movement in the counterclockwise direction is a positive
column rotation. Movement in the clockwise direction is a negative column rotation.

3.3 Description of the treatment table assembly
The treatment table assembly has five primary parts:
• Longitudinal/lateral (X/Y) carriage assemblies
• Height (Z) mechanism
• Column rotation assembly
• Isocentric rotation assembly
• Treatment table top.

Product description
Description of the treatment table assembly
1
2
3
4
000419 ©2010 Elekta Limited
Figure 3.10 Primary parts of the treatment table assembly
(1) Longitudinal/lateral carriage (3) Column rotation assembly
assemblies
(2) Height mechanism (4) Isocentric rotation assembly
The iso-rotation assembly connects to the column rotation assembly, and the iso-rotation drive
mechanism is installed in a shallow pit in the floor of the treatment room. The iso-rotation
assembly therefore holds all the weight of the treatment table assembly. The isocentric rotation
assembly rotates the treatment table assembly, and the treatment table top, about the axis of
the host machine isocenter. The column rotation assembly connects between the isocentric
rotation assembly and the height mechanism. The column rotation assembly rotates the height
mechanism, the longitudinal/lateral carriage assembly and the treatment table top about the
column axis.
The height mechanism connects between the column rotation assembly and the longitudinal/
lateral carriage assembly. The height mechanism lifts and lowers the longitudinal/lateral carriage
assembly and the treatment table top. The longitudinal/lateral carriage assemblies connect
between the height mechanism and the treatment table top. The longitudinal carriage assembly
moves the treatment table top in the G and T directions along the horizontal axis. The lateral
carriage assembly moves the treatment table top in the A and B directions along the horizontal
axis.

3.4 Configuration and function of the user interface modules
The treatment table has two user interface modules, one on the A-side and one on the B-side.
Each user interface module has these controls:
• Brake buttons
• TOUCHGUARD OVERRIDE button
• Thumbwheels
• STOP MOTORS button
• ENABLE bars.
The brake buttons are toggle action devices, which contain lamps to show the position of the
brake. The brake of each movement system is usually in the engaged position, and the lamp is
on. When the brake releases, the lamp goes off.
The TOUCHGUAD OVERRIDE buttons are momentary action devices, which contain lamps to
show the condition of the touchguard inhibit. The touchguard override function is usually off (the
button is in the off position), and the lamp is on. The user must hold the button in the on position
to override the touchguard inhibits. In this position, the lamp goes off.
There are four thumbwheels on the user interface modules which control the speed and
direction of the movement. Move the thumbwheel up or down to select the direction of travel.
Move the thumbwheel away from the center position to increase the speed of movement in the
selected direction. Release the thumbwheel to stop the selected movement.
Use the ENABLE bar at the same time as one of the four thumbwheels to enable movement. The
treatment table moves in the direction that you operate the thumbwheel. Release the ENABLE
bar after you release the selected thumbwheel and the selected movement stops.
The cover of the user interface module is in two parts (front and rear) that you can disassemble
to get access to the internal parts. Most of the internal parts are installed in the front part of the
cover. There are two PCBs in the user interface module. One PCB one holds the potentiometer
thumbwheels and the other PCB holds the switches and indicators of the buttons. The wires for
the user interface modules come from PCB 830B on the side of the height mechanism.
The treatment table can have one of these two types of user interface module:
• Standard configuration, where one button releases the lateral and longitudinal brakes
(see Figure 3.11)
• Configuration for an independently operated longitudinal and lateral brake system,
where there is one button for the lateral brake and one button for the longitudinal
brake (see Figure 3.12)
A modification kit (CN50-0162) is available to change the standard configuration to the
configuration for an independently operated longitudinal and lateral brake system.

3.5 Configuration and function of the function keypad
There are two types of function keypad (FKP) for the treatment table, a new FKP (see Figure 3.13)
and a legacy FKP (see Figure 3.14). The new FKP has more dedicated function keys than the
legacy FKP. The legends of the related figure show the functions of the keys on the related FKP.
The keys on the FKP start, interrupt and terminate radiation, control the ASU functions and page
through the optional monitor display.
The selection of the TABLE ASU function is different for each FKP. On the new FKP, operate the
ENABLE and TABLE ASU keys together to start the TABLE ASU function. On the legacy FKP,
operate the AUTO and + keys (known as TABLE ASU) together to start the TABLE ASU function.

3.6 Description of the treatment table covers
The treatment table has an outer set of covers and a set of covers for the longitudinal and lateral
carriage assemblies. The outer covers are the:
• Top cover
• Bellows cover
• Base cover.
The covers for the the longitudinal and lateral carriage assemblies each have:
• Two end fingertrap cover plates, one at each end of the treatment table
• Two side fingertrap cover plates, one on each side of the treatment table
• One middle fingertrap cover plate.
The function of the covers is to:
• Prevent injury to patients, operators, and engineers from the movement of some parts
of the treatment table
• Prevent damage to some parts of the treatment table during operation.

3.7 Description of the treatment table tops
3.7.1 Standard therapy table top
The standard therapy treatment table lets you deliver radiation from most directions without
barriers. It has a Mylar® cover and small and large panels that you can remove. The panels have
labels to help you to attach them in the correct position after removal. The rails on the sides let
you attach accessories.
3.7.2 C-Arm table top
The C-Arm therapy table top has a panel and accessory rails that you can remove. The panel can
be solid or it can have a tennis racket design with a carbon fiber or metal frame. Panels with a
carbon fiber frame give better X-ray translucence. The tennis racket design gives good patient
support with minimum absorbed radiation.
There are two C-shaped arms below the panel. Each arm can move through 180°, and there are
six stop positions 30° apart. Extension panels attach to the ends of the treatment table top to
give support to the head or feet of the patient.

3.10 Description and operation of the longitudinal and lateral
movement assemblies
3.10.1 Technical description of the longitudinal and lateral carriage
assemblies
The X/Y carriage assembly supplies longitudinal and lateral movement of the treatment table top.
The rails of the X/Y carriage assembly are installed in the roller bearing cages of the chassis. The
longitudinal rails of the X/Y carriage assembly are installed in the longitudinal roller bearing
cages. The lateral rails of the X/Y carriage assembly are installed in the lateral roller bearing
cages. The height interface plate is attached to the top of the height mechanism.
A DC motor moves the X/Y carriage assemblies along the related roller bearing cages. The
LATERAL and LONGITUDINAL thumbwheels on the user interface module control the operation of
the DC motor. The thumbwheels control the speed and direction of movement of the related
carriage assembly.

A toothed drive roller installs on the end of the drive shaft of the DC motor in each drive
subassembly. A drive belt is in contact with the drive roller and a set of potentiometer drive
gears. When the DC motor energizes, the clutch engages to rotate the drive roller and disengages
when the movement is completed. The drive roller turns the potentiometer drive gears and the
drive belt. The polarity of the drive signal to the DC motor sets the direction of movement. The
amplitude of the drive signal sets the speed of movement.
The two drive subassemblies share one interconnection printed circuit board (PCB830A) that is
installed on the height mechanism. The PCB supplies cable connections for the DC motor, brake
and clutch and position readout potentiometers (coarse, fine and check potentiometers).
The positional readout circuit has a coarse potentiometer, a fine potentiometer and a check
potentiometer. See Movement servo loops for more information.

3.10.3 Technical description of the longitudinal and lateral brake
assemblies
The longitudinal and lateral brake assemblies stop the movement of the related carriage
assemblies. These brake assemblies are installed on the bottom surface of the related carriage
assembly. Each brake assembly has upper and lower steel brake-bands attached to mounting
blocks at each end. The brake-bands are installed between four layers of friction material. The
two screws between the mounting blocks at each end, adjust the brake-band tension (see Figure
3.22 and Figure 3.23).

Spring pressure usually holds each brake assembly in the on position. Operation of the LAT/LONG
BRAKE (or LATERAL BRAKE or LONGITUDINAL BRAKE) button on the user interface module,
applies a 52 V electrical supply to energize the coil of the solenoid. The coil compresses the spring
to release the brake. A 26 V electrical supply holds the brake in the released position. Operation
of the LAT/LONG BRAKE (or LATERAL BRAKE or LONGITUDINAL BRAKE) button again removes the
26 V supply to the coil of the solenoid. The coil de-energizes and the spring extends to apply the
related brake.

The chassis of the height mechanism has an upper and lower rectangular arm and a three-angle
box. The upper and lower arms attach to top and bottom interface plates. The bottom of the
upper arm and the top of the lower arm attach to the three-angle box to make a pivot point.
A DC motor moves a worm drive which operates a ball screw to move the height mechanism
vertically. When you increase the height of the treatment table, the upper and lower arms
extend. When you decrease the height of the treatment table, the upper and lower arms retract.
The height mechanism has an upper and a lower limit switch. The limit switches operate to
prevent movement of the treatment table if it moves out of the defined range of movement.
The height mechanism has two interconnection printed circuit boards (PCBs 825A and 825B)
installed on the T face of the three-angle box. The PCBs supply cable connections for the DC

3.11.2 Technical description of the height mechanism drive motor
The height mechanism drive motor operates the gearbox assembly to increase or decrease the
height of the treatment table. The drive motor is installed on the gearbox in the three-angle box.
The drive motor operates the worm drive of the gearbox assembly, which extends or retracts the
upper and lower ball screws. The amplitude of the drive signal to the motor sets the speed of
movement, and the polarity of the signal gives the direction of movement. Spring pressure
applies the brake of the drive motor when no height movement is selected. A height movement
selection automatically releases the brake.
The HEIGHT thumbwheel on the user interface module controls the operation of the drive motor.
Operation of the thumbwheel controls the speed and direction of movement of the height
mechanism.

3.11.3 Technical description of the gearbox assembly of the height
mechanism
The height mechanism gearbox assembly extends or retracts the upper and lower arms of the
height mechanism to lift or lower the treatment table (see Figure 3.26). The gearbox mountings
install in a linear bearing and a roller bearing. The roller bearing is installed on the A-side bracket
and the linear bearing on the B-side bracket of the three-angle box. The gearbox assembly moves
horizontally in the bearings.
The gearbox assembly has:
• A gearbox
• An upper and lower ball screw
• An upper and lower ball screw cup (see the Technical description of the height
mechanism drive motor for more information).

Product description
Description and operation of the height movement assemblies
The roller bearing has a ball race that moves horizontally along the roller bearing guide. A special
M12 bolt and spacer washer attach the ball race to the gearbox.
The linear bearing is a machined guide block with a channel in the center where the linear
bearings move. Eight M4 × 16 mm cap-head screws hold the guide block in position. The screws
go through the applicable holes in the linear bearing guide plate and the B-side bracket of the
center casting, to the screw thread holes in the guide block. The linear bearing has four linear
bearings and no shims (see Figure 3.26).

The gearbox assembly has a worm drive connected through a coupling housing to two rotating
nuts. The height mechanism motor drive operates the worm drive. The worm drive turns the two
rotating nuts at the same time and at the same speed. This synchronizes the movement of the
ball screws, which do not rotate, through the gearbox assembly. One rotating nut moves the
upper ball screw and the other rotating nut moves the lower ball screw. The rotating nuts change
the rotation of the worm drive into vertical movement of the ball screws.
Ball screw cups are attached to the end of the ball screws and to the upper and lower arms. A
high-tensile screw attaches the ball screw cup to the end of the ball screw. A pivot pin installs
through needle bearings to attach a ball screw cup to each arm. A screw locks the pivot pin in
position. The ball screw and ball screw cup rotate together on the needle bearings around the
pivot pin axis (see Figure 3.27).

3.12.1 Technical description of the iso-rotation assembly
The iso-rotation assembly lets you rotate the treatment table about the iso-center (I axis) of the
digital accelerator. The iso-rotation assembly installs on the floor of the treatment table pit.
The primary parts of the iso-rotation assembly are the:
• Triangular base
• Column/iso-rotation interface arm
• Iso-rotation drive subassembly
• Drive platter

The triangular base of the iso-rotation assembly attaches to five mountings installed on the floor
of the treatment table pit, which gives a rigid structure. The height of the triangular base is set on
installation.
The iso-rotation drive subassembly and the drive platter are on the triangular base. A toothed
drive belt installs around the edge of the drive platter and engages with the toothed drive gears
of the iso-rotation drive subassembly. The drive platter has a hole in the center. The column/iso-
rotation interface arm is opposite the iso-rotation drive subassembly and is permanently
attached to the drive platter.
The iso-rotation assembly has two interconnection printed circuit boards (PCBs 811 and 812). The
PCBs supply cable connections for the DC motor, brake and clutch and the position readout
potentiometers. The coarse, check and fine potentiometers are installed on the iso-rotation drive
subassembly.

The ISO-ROTATION thumbwheel on the user interface controls the operation of the iso-rotation
assembly. The ISO-ROTATION BRAKE button applies and releases the iso-rotation brake assembly.
The iso-rotation drive subassembly moves the treatment table through its full range of
movement.

3.12.2 Technical description of the iso-rotation drive subassembly
The iso-rotation drive subassembly (see Figure 3.29) rotates the treatment table around the
isocentric axis through a 100° range of movement. The iso-rotation drive subassembly installs on
the triangular base of the iso-rotation assembly at the gun side of the drive platter.
The primary parts of the iso-rotation drive subassembly are the:
• DC motor and clutch
• Upper and lower drive gears
• Timing belt.
The iso-rotation drive subassembly has a pillar and drive shaft installed between a top and a
lower plate. The DC motor and clutch unit installs vertically between the top and lower plates.
The drive shaft of the DC motor extends through the lower plate. The drive shaft engages in a
pair of lower drive gears installed on the external surface of the lower plate.
A toothed timing belt installed around the lower drive gears rotates the drive gears. The drive
shaft installed between the lower drive gears and upper drive gears rotates the upper drive
gears. A toothed drive belt, installed around the edge of the drive platter, engages on the upper
drive gears and moves the drive platter to the selected position. The polarity of the drive signal to
the DC motor sets the direction of movement. The amplitude of the drive signal sets the speed of
movement.
The ISO-ROTATION thumbwheel on the user interface module controls the operation of the DC
motor. Operation of the thumbwheel controls the speed and direction of movement of the iso-
rotation assembly, and releases the ISO-ROTATION brake.
The ISO-ROTATION BRAKE button on the user interface module manually controls the operation
of the iso-rotation brake. A usually de-energized electromagnet holds the electrically operated
brake in contact with the floor pan (ON). Operation of the ISO-ROTATION BRAKE button applies a
26 V supply to energize the solenoid of the electromagnet, which releases the brake. Operation
of the ISO-ROTATION BRAKE button again removes the 26 V supply to the coil of the
electromagnet, which de-energizes to apply the brake.

3.12.3 Technical description of the floor parts of the iso-rotation assembly
The floor parts of the iso-rotation assembly give protection to the drive mechanism. The floor
parts are installed on mountings on the floor of the treatment table pit.
The primary parts of the floor are the:
• Floor pan
• Floorboards.
The floor pan is a disk that has a location pin in the center. The location pin engages in the hole in
the center of the drive platter. A location pin on the column rotation assembly engages in a slot
in the floor pan. The floor pan rotates through an approximate 200° range of movement, 100° at
each side of the longitudinal axis.

Product description
Description and operation of the iso-rotation assembly
A mechanical scale installed around the outer top surface of the floor pan gives a visual indication
of the rotation of the treatment table in degrees. The graduations on the two faces of the scale
are from different scale conventions. The graduations on one face of the scale are for the
IEC 61217 convention and on the other face for the IEC 60601 convention. Therefore, you can
change the scale convention for the iso-rotation indications.
Four floorboard quadrants are installed around the floor pan on five mountings. An isocentric
arrow symbol attached to the floorboards shows the angular rotation of the treatment table
against the scale. The removal of the floorboard quadrants gives access to the iso-rotation
assembly.

3.12.4 Technical description and operation of the column rotation
assembly
The column rotation assembly is a casting that holds the circular bearing on which the height
mechanism is installed. It has an electrical brake unit, with a magnet coil, that is usually in the
engaged position. There is one interconnection PCB (PCB 820) on the column rotation assembly.
The PCB connects to the cables for the movement readout circuit, which includes the coarse,

Product description
Description and operation of the power supply unit
3.14.2 Function of the mains input circuit of the power supply unit
The mains electrical power supply of 230 V (±15%) to the PSU comes from the UPS. There is an
amber neon lamp (N1) connected across the live and neutral lines that shows the condition of
the mains input.
The live line goes across a contact of circuit breaker CB1 to the primary winding of transformer
T1. Circuit breaker CB1 is a double-pole, 5 A device that isolates the primary side of transformer
T1 from the mains electrical power supply. The neutral line goes to the primary winding of T1
through the negative temperature coefficient (NTC) thermistors on the LED PCB, which gives
protection from power surges. The mains transformer (T1) is a 1.2 kVA toroid, with an internal
thermal switch that operates if the transformer becomes too hot because of unusual,
continuous, high load conditions or an internal fault.
The transformer has two secondary windings. At a nominal input of 230 V AC:
• Secondary winding Sy1 gives an output of 80 V rms to the 90 V line.
• Secondary winding Sy2 gives outputs of 20 V, 0 V, and 20 V rms to the +26 V and -26 V
lines.
The 80 V rms output from secondary winding Sy1 of transformer T1 is full-wave rectified by REC1
and smoothed by capacitor C1. The 0 V output from secondary winding Sy2 is grounded. The 20 V
outputs from Sy2 are full-wave rectified by REC2 and smoothed by C2 and C3 to give +26 V DC
and -26 V DC.
3.14.3 Function of the discharge PCB in the power supply unit
The discharge PCB is installed above capacitor C1 in the PSU. The PCB is in the 90 V supply line,
where its function is to make sure that C1 discharges to a safe level in 15 seconds or less. If there
is a voltage, an LED (D1) comes on.
Note: The safe level gives protection only from the risk from an electric shock. More than 15 seconds is
necessary to discharge all the energy.
The discharge PCB also contains a potential divider that gives a lower voltage to the LED PCB. This
voltage causes an LED to come on to show the condition of the switched 90 V output.
3.14.4 Function of the heatsink PCB in the power supply unit
The heatsink PCB has a current dump circuit to keep the ±26 V outputs to less than 1.7 V of the
supply level. To do this, switching devices (TR1 and TR2) come on when the output voltage
increases, and resistor packages (TO220 type) on the heatsink dump the current. The output of
the current dump circuit goes to the LED PCB through the contacts of CON A and CON B.
The heatsink PCB also has three voltage regulators. The rectified ±26 V signals go to the
regulators to give +5 V and ±15 V at 1.5 A (peak). A dropper resistor reduces the heat dissipation
of the +5 V regulator.
3.14.5 Configuration and function of the LED PCB in the power supply unit
The LED PCB has a number of LEDs that show on the front panel of the PSU. Each LED shows the
condition of a supply output. The LED will come on if there is a supply output, but will go off if
there is no supply output.
On the LED PCB, the +26 V and -26 V inputs from the heatsink PCB divide into three lines (lines 1,
2, and 3) which give six output lines. The PCB has a set of PTC fuses that automatically reset to
Document ID: 45133701862 10 Precise Treatment Table™

2, and 3. The PCB also continuously supplies ±26 V to the UMD PCBs of the gantry motor drive.
7.5 A PTC fuses that automatically reset give protection to these supplies.

3.14.6 Function of the interlock circuits in the LED PCB
The interlock circuits have a set of relays, which control signal inputs energize to show the
interlock status of the host machine and treatment table. When all interlock conditions are
satisfactory, the contacts of the relays close and the circuit applies +26 V to the coils of
contactors CON A and CON B.
If there is an interruption in the interlock chain, the +26 V power supply stops for approximately
5 seconds. This makes sure that the brakes are applied and that all treatment table movements
stop when the interruption occurs.

3.14.7 Function of the 90 V output signal of the power supply unit
The rectified and smoothed 90 V power line goes through the contacts of CON A and CON B, and
fuse FS1, to socket SK802N. Fuse FS1 is located on the front panel of the PSU and is a 10.0 A
(rated at 250 V) 32 × 6.3 mm glass time delay fuse. It gives protection to the circuit from output
short-circuits and overloads.
For de-rating, there are four switched outputs at SK802N:
• Pin 9 +90V#1
• Pin 8 +90V#2
• Pin 7 +90V#3
• Pin 6 +90V#4.
There are four grounded 90 V return signals:
• Pin 5 0V(90V)#1
• Pin 4 0V(90V)#2
• Pin 2 0V(90V)#3
• Pin 1 0V(90V)#4.
Related Links:
Configuration and function of the power supply unit on page 60
3.14.8 Function of the ±26 V DC output signal of the power supply unit
The rectified and smoothed ±26 V power lines go to the LED PCB through the heatsink PCB and
the contacts of CON A and CON B. On the LED PCB, the -26 V input divides into three lines. Each
lines gets protection from a 3A thermal auto-resetting fuse.
The +26 V input divides into four lines, each with protection from a 3 A thermal auto-resetting
fuse. In one +26 V line, two auto-resetting PTC fuses are connected in parallel to give 6 A fuse
protection at SK802P, pin 12 (+26V#1). A single auto-resetting PTC fuse gives 3 A fuse protection
at SK802P, pin 6 (-26V#1).
Additional 26 V supply is generated to power the gantry motor drive UMD PCBs. These supplies
are protected with 7.5 A auto-resetting PTC fuses. The supplies are output through CON C to
SK802P.
The ±26 V power outputs at SK820P are:
• Pin 12 +26V#1
• Pin 10 +26V#2
• Pin 11 +26V#3
• Pin 24 +26V unswitched (gantry motor drive)
• Pin 6 -26V#1

3.15 Description and operation of the uninterruptable power
supply
3.15.1 Configuration of the uninterruptible power supply
There are two types of UPS. The UPS 45133318015 is in the EIM cabinet and the UPS 1515524 is
in the position adjacent to the EIM cabinet. Both of them are connected to the PSU. The
operation of the UPS differs if the treatment table includes an Emergency Down Drive (EDD)
timer extension module.
The UPS supplies power if a mains power supply failure occurs. This gives sufficient operating
time for the user to lower the table safely.
There are two different configurations of the UPS:
• UPS with EDD timer extension module. (This is standard installation on digital
accelerators with Serial No. 152530 or earlier).
• UPS without EDD timer extension module. (This is standard installation on digital
accelerators with Serial No. 152531 or later).
The EDD module is available as a ModKit (Part No. 8213 215 8674)
Note: When there is mains power supply to the treatment table, the UPS can give an audible alarm. The
audible alarm can occur when you move the treatment table up. The audible alarm is not an
indication of a system fault if the mains power supply is available.

3.15.2 Configuration and function of the UPS without the emergency down
drive timer module
The PSU controls the operation of the UPS and sends a shutdown signal to the UPS through the
backplane. This prevents the backup operation of the UPS in standard conditions of operation.
The PSU shuts down the UPS as shown in Figure 3.35.

(not TEST)
002013 ©2011 Elekta Limited
Figure 3.35 UPS shutdown conditions
Shut down of the UPS takes approximately 90 seconds.
If the UPS TEST switch on the front panel of the PSU is set to the TEST position, a red test warning
LED illuminates and the UPS maintains power after the external mains supply isolator has been
tripped.
The integrity of the mains power supply is monitored by a 24 V AC input that energizes relay RL1.
The contacts of RL1 close to connect +26 V, via a resistor and zener diode, to the UPS shut–down
input, when the UPS TEST switch is in the NORMAL position.
If the 24 V AC host power supply is lost, or when the UPS TEST switch is set to the TEST position, a
switched outputs contactor is enabled by the assertion of stop motors signals STMPSS (24 V DC)
and STMLCU (4 V DC) only.
Note: The UPS maintains its output whilst the mains input is present, even though a shut–down request
is in force. To ensure UPS shutdown the shut–down request must be in force for at least
0.5 seconds after the cessation of the mains supply.

3.15.3 Configuration and function of the UPS with the emergency down
drive timer module
The UPS with an EDD lets the user lower the treatment table to a safe height up to five minutes
after the system has been shutdown.
The treatment table descends when the movement ENABLE bar on a user interface module is
pressed within five minutes of the shutdown of the system.
If there is a mains power supply failure, the EIM PSU will continue to operate until the UPS
battery is fully discharged.

3.16 Description and operation of the level translator unit PCB
3.16.1 Configuration and function of the level translator unit PCB
The LTU is the electrical interface for the signals between the LCU PCB and the:
• Digital accelerator
• Movement subsystems
• User interfaces modules
• Other equipment.
The LTU PCB changes (translates) the levels of its input signals to give an output at the necessary
levels to interface with the treatment table and the digital accelerator.
The PCB has a number of circuits which give these functions.
• Reference voltage level translation
• Power decoupling
• Digital level translation of brake control signals
• Digital level translation of clutch control signals
• Analog level translation of speed demand signals
• Analog level translation of potentiometer readout signals
• Motor drive enable signal processing
• Height motor current monitor signal processing
• Interlock control level translation and processing
• User interface lamp control signal level translation

The power supply circuit of the LTU PCB supplies the power rails of the LTU PCB with:

3.16.4 Level translation of the reference voltage signals on the LTU PCB
Signal VREFALCU is the A-side input reference voltage from the LCU PCB. Signal VREFALCU goes
through a buffer amplifier (IC48 10/11/13) to operate an integrating controller (IC28 5/6/7 and
C97). These devices operate the gate of a p-channel MOSFET (TR1), which increases the current
level of reference voltage +10VREFA at the source pin of the MOSFET.
The +10VREFA line is the output from the LTU PCB (PL1 A8/B8/C8) that goes to amplifiers (IC29
12/13/14 and 2/3/1) to get local A-side reference voltages -1VR, +1VR, -5VR, and +5VR. These
voltages are used for digital and analog level translation.
Signal VREFBLCU is the B-side input reference voltage from the LCU PCB. Signal VREFBLCU goes
through a buffer amplifier (IC48 8/9/14) to operate an integrating controller (IC43/IC41 and IC22/
Document ID: 45133701862 10 Precise Treatment Table™
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 70 ©2019 Elekta Limited.

Product description
Description and operation of the level translator unit PCB
C134). These devices operate the gate of a p-channel MOSFET (TR3), which gives a reference
voltage output +10VREFB. The +10VREFB signal goes to amplifier IC32 9/10/8 to give signal
VREF10N, and to amplifier IC32 6/5/7 to give signal VREF0V5N.
Input signal 10VAHOST1 from the host machine shows if the host machine is connected. The
filtered signal goes to a comparator (IC39 3/2/7) to give the active high signal FLG. The FLG signal
goes through an inverting buffer (IC27/8) to give signal FLGHOST1LTU, which is an active-high
signal. This signal goes through a second inverting buffer (IC27 11/9) to give the active low signal
FLGHOST1LTU.
3.16.5 Function of the brake signal circuit of the level translation unit PCB
The brake signal circuit of the level translation unit PCB decodes the analog input signals from the
user interfaces. The circuit sends commands to release the lateral or longitudinal brake. If the
treatment table has independently operated lateral and longitudinal brake systems, the circuit
decodes the input signals differently (see Figure 3.38 and Figure 3.39).

The input SWDPSS is an analog signal in the range 0 V to 5.2 V from the brake release buttons of
the user interfaces. The circuit buffers the signal (IC38 6/5/7) and changes it to an 8-bit digital
word (DB0 to DB7) in an ADC (IC31). The digital word goes to the EPROM (IC33) which sends an
output (D0 to D7) command to release the brake. The look-up tables (LUTs) that follow show the

Figure 3.40 Level translation of the brake control signals
In the iso-rotation system, the active low signal BRKILCU from the LCU PCB controls the manual
brake release. The signal goes to a CMOS inverter (IC51 3/4) that directly operates a smart
MOSFET power switch (TR17). The output from the MOSFET is the power enable signal BRKILTU,
which applies +26 V and 0 V across the coil of the brake to release it.
In the height movement system, the active low signal BRKZLCU from the LCU PCB controls the
manual brake release. The signal goes to a CMOS inverter (IC51 1/2) that directly operates a
smart MOSFET power switch (TR16). The output from the MOSFET is the power enable signal
BRKZLTU, which applies +26 V and 0 V across the coil of the brake to release it.
In the column movement system, the active low signal BRKCLCU from the LCU PCB controls the
manual brake release. The signal goes to a CMOS inverter (IC51 5/6) that directly operates a
smart MOSFET power switch (TR18). The output from the MOSFET is the power enable signal
BRKCLTU, which applies +26 V and 0 V across the coil of the brake to release it.
In the longitudinal movement system, signal BRKXLCU from the LCU PCB controls the manual
brake release. The signal goes to a CMOS inverter (IC51 11/10) which sends the signal to a
monostable (IC46). A comparator (IC49 10/11/13) uses the output of the inverter to operate the
gate of a p-channel MOSFET (TR7). The +26 V supply at the source pin of the MOSFET switches to
the drain pin, which applies the +26 V to one pin of the coil. The the other pin is permanently
connected to –26 V. Therefore, the power enable signal BRKXLTU applies +52 V across the coil of
the brake to release it.
After the trigger pulse, the MOSFET switches off, and the high signal from the monostable clocks
a flip flop (IC47 1 to 5). The high output signal of the flip flop operates the gate of the MOSFET
(TR8) through an amplifier (IC50 5/5/2). This switches on TR8, which applies 0 V and -26 V across
the coil of the brake. The length of the initial high pulse is adjustable, through R277 and C165.
In the lateral movement system, signal BRKYLCU from the LCU PCB controls the manual brake
release. The signal goes to a CMOS inverter (IC51 13/12) which sends the signal to a monostable

channel MOSFET (TR13). The +26 V supply at the source pin of the MOSFET switches to the drain
pin, which applies the +26 V to one pin of the coil. The the other pin is permanently connected to
–26 V. Therefore, the power enable signal BRKYLTU applies +52 V across the coil of the brake to
release it.
After the trigger pulse, the MOSFET switches off, and the high signal from the monostable clocks
a flip flop (IC47 9 to 13). The high output signal of the flip flop operates the gate of the MOSFET

across the coil of the brake. The length of the initial high pulse is adjustable, through R295 and
C175.

In the iso-rotation system, the active low signal CLUILCU from the LCU PCB controls the operation
of the clutch. The signal goes to a CMOS inverter (IC51 9/8) that directly operates a smart
MOSFET power switch (TR15). The output from the MOSFET is the power enable signal CLUILTU,
which applies +26 V and 0 V across the coil of the clutch to operate it.
In the longitudinal movement system, the active low signal CLUXLCU from the LCU PCB controls
the operation of the clutch. The signal goes to two comparators (IC49 4/5 and IC50 10/11) that
operate the gate of a p-channel MOSFET (TR11). The output from the MOSFET is the power
enable signal CLUXLTU, which applies +26 V and 0 V across the coil of the clutch to operate it.
In the lateral movement system, the active low signal CLUYLCU from the LCU PCB controls the
operation of the clutch. The signal goes to two comparators (IC49 6/7 and IC50 8/9) that operate
the gate of a p-channel MOSFET (TR14). The output from the MOSFET is the power enable signal
CLUYLTU, which applies +26 V and 0 V across the coil of the clutch to operate it.

In the longitudinal movement system, the input signal CRSXPSS is the readback signal from the
wiper of the coarse potentiometer of the longitudinal movement system. The signal, which is in
the range 0 V to +10 V, goes to a buffer amplifier (IC1 2/3/1). The buffered signal then goes to a
linear amplifier (IC1 6/5/7), which does the necessary level translation. The signal then goes to an
adjustable ±1 gain buffer (IC7 9/10/8), to which you can apply link LK2 to set the gain to unity.
The output signal CRSXLTU from the circuit goes to the host machine.
The buffered coarse potentiometer signal also goes to a differentiator (IC1 13/12/14) that gives
an output voltage signal in proportion to the speed. The voltage signal goes to a ±1 gain buffer
(IC7 13/12/14 with link LK1), which sends the velocity feedback signal VFBXLTU to the LCU PCB.
The level translation of the speed voltage to a unipolar voltage (IC1 9/10/8) gives the output
signal VFBXLTULCU to the LCU PCB.
In the lateral movement system, the input signal CRSYPSS is the readback signal from the wiper
of the coarse potentiometer of the lateral movement system. The signal, which is in the range 0 V
to +10 V, goes to a buffer amplifier (IC4 2/3/1). The buffered signal then goes to a linear amplifier

In the longitudinal movement system, the motor drive signal ENBXLCU from the LCU PCB goes
through a resistive voltage divider (R219/R220) to a Schmitt-triggered NAND gate (IC30
12/13/11). Together, the voltage divider and the gate translate the +26 V input level of signal
ENBXLCU to a +5 V digital level. The other input to the NAND gate is the CONFIRM signal from the
interlock level translation logic, which shows that the host machine is enabled.
The output of the NAND gate goes directly to the LCU PCB as an active low signal ENBXLTU. The
output of the NAND gate goes to a comparator (IC35 10/11/13), which translates the signal level
from +5 V to +15 V. This output is the motor drive enable signal EMBXLTUXMD that goes to the X-
UMD PCB.
In the lateral movement system, the motor drive signal ENBYLCU from the LCU PCB goes through
a resistive voltage divider (R245/R246) to a Schmitt-triggered NAND gate (IC30 4/5/6). Together,
the voltage divider and the gate translate the +26 V input level of signal ENBYLCU to a +5 V digital
level. The other input to the NAND gate is the CONFIRM signal from the interlock level translation
logic, which shows that the host machine is enabled.
The output of the NAND gate goes directly to the LCU PCB as an active low signal ENBYLTU. The
output of the NAND gate goes to a comparator (IC35 4/5/2), which translates the signal level
from +5 V to +15 V. This output is the motor drive enable signal EMBYLTUYMD that goes to the Y-

level. The other input to the NAND gate is the CONFIRM signal from the interlock level translation
logic, which shows that the host machine is enabled.
The output of the NAND gate goes directly to the LCU PCB as an active low signal ENBZLTU. The

3.18.1 Level translation of the interlock control signals on the LTU PCB
The LTU PCB receives safety interlock signals from the treatment table, the user interface
modules, the digital accelerator, and other equipment in the treatment table control system. On
the LTU PCB, logic circuits process the signals to give an indication of the interlock status to the
LCU PCB. Where necessary, resistive voltage dividers and Schmitt-triggered gates digitally
translate the signals from from +26 V to +5 V. The circuits use comparators for digital translation
from +5 V to +15 V or +26 V, and electromechanical relays for hard-wired interlock signals.

3.18.2 Configuration of the interlock control circuits on the LTU PCB
The interlock control circuit on the LTU PCB has a two switches (SW1 and SW2) on the backplane.
With these switches, service engineers can disconnect the treatment table from the host
machine (or other equipment) to put it in stand-alone mode. When the contacts of the switches
are open, they disconnect some of the signals to and from the host machine and the other
equipment.
Note: Stand-alone operation is not possible if PL801M is connected at the backplane.

3.18.3 Operation and analysis of the touchguard interlock control signals
From the digital accelerator, signal TGFHOST1 is the active low touchguard fault indication signal.
It is an AC voltage signal from the movements interlock circuits of the digital accelerator.
Resistive voltage dividers (R169/R170) divide the signal, and two operational amplifiers (IC21
2/3/1 and IC21 6/5/7) rectify it, before the signal goes through a NAND gate with the flag signal
FLGHOST1LTU (IC20 12/13/11).
Signal TGFHOST2 from other equipment, is an active low touchguard fault indication. Resistive
voltage dividers (R163/R164) divide the signal before it goes through a NAND gate (IC20/1) with
flag signal FLGHOST1LTU. The gated signal TGFHOST2 goes through a second NAND gate with the
TGFHOST1 signal (IC20 9/10/8 and 4/5/6).
The result of this logic circuit is a NOR function, in which TGFHOST1 or TGFHOST2 can give the
touchguard flag signal TGFLTU. The touchguard flag signal TGFLTU goes through an inverting
buffer (IC27/2) that sends the active low output signal TGFLTU to the LCU PCB.

3.18.4 Operation and analysis of the touchguard override and velocity
inhibit signals
Signal TGOPSS is the touchguard override signal from the TOUCHGUARD OVERRIDE button of the
user interface. Resistive voltage dividers (R187/R188) divide the signal, and the operational
amplifiers (IC22 1/2) invert it to give the output signal TGOLTU to the LCU PCB.
The output signal TGOLTU goes through a NAND gate (IC23 1/2/3) with the touchguard flag
TGFLTU. The output goes through a second NAND gate (IC23 4/5/6) with signal CONENPSS from
the configuration selection circuit.
The result of this logic circuit is a velocity inhibit signal VELINHLTU. The velocity inhibit signal
VELINHLTU goes through an inverting buffer (IC27) that sends the active low output signal
VELINHLTU to the LCU PCB.

3.18.5 Operation and analysis of the HT interlock signal
Signal HTINTHOST is the HT enable signal from the digital accelerator. When it is high, the signal
shows a fault in the HT system. Resistive voltage dividers (R192/R179) divide the signal down,
and operational amplifiers (IC22 11/7/10) invert it, before it goes to an inverting buffer (IC27) as
signal HTINTLTU. The output of the buffer is the active high signal HTINTLTU to the LCU PCB.

3.18.6 Operation and analysis of the motor enable interlock signal
Signal ENBZHOST is the height motor enable signal from the digital accelerator. Resistive voltage
dividers (R201/202) divide the active low signal, before it goes through a Schmitt-triggered NAND
gate (IC26 12/13/11). The other input to the gate is the divided (R195/R197) and inverted (IC24
13/12) height motor enable signal ENBZHOST2 from the other equipment. The output from the
NAND gate is signal ENBZHOSTLTU, which goes to an inverting buffer (IC27). The output of the
buffer is the height motor enable signal ENBZHOSTLTU to the LCU PCB.
Note: Signal ENBZHOSTLTU also goes through a NOR gate (IC25/1), with the longitudinal and lateral
motor enable signals, to give a PSU enable signal that is not used in this application.
Signal ENBYHOST is the lateral motor enable signal from the digital accelerator. Resistive voltage
dividers (R217/R218) divide the signal, and operational amplifiers (IC24 11/10) invert it, before it
goes through a NOR gate (IC25/2). The second input to the NOR gate (IC25/13) is the divided
(R222/R223) and inverted (IC24 9/8) longitudinal motor enable signal ENBXHOST from the digital
accelerator. The third input to the gate is signal ENBZHOSTLTU.
The output of the NOR gate (IC25 1/2/13) gives the interlock status of the height, longitudinal,
and lateral motor enable inputs. The inverted (IC24 5/6) output signal goes through a second
NOR gate (IC25/3), which gates the signal with the iso-rotation motor and common movement
enable signals.
Signal ENBIHOST is the iso-rotation motor enable signal from the digital accelerator. Resistive
voltage dividers (R227/R228) divide the signal, and operational amplifiers (IC24 1/2) invert it. The
signal goes through a NOR gate (IC25/4), which gates the signal with the longitudinal and lateral
motor enable signals.

3.18.7 Operation and analysis of the movement enable interlock signal
Signal ENBPSS is the movement enable signal from the ENABLE bar of the user interface. Resistive
voltage dividers (R234/R235) divide the active low signal, and operational amplifiers (IC24 3/4)
invert it. The signal goes through a NOR gate (IC25/5), which gates the signal with the
longitudinal, lateral, and iso-rotation motor enable signals. (This logic gives an input to the stop
motors gating circuit.) A NAND gate (IC26 9/10/8) inverts the output of the NOR gate (IC25/6).
The output from the NAND gate is the CONFIRM signal for the level translation of the motor
enable signal.
Note: The output signal ENBPSSLTU of the divided signal ENBPSS is not used in this application.

3.18.8 Operation and analysis of the stop motors and mains power supply
failure signals
Input signal STMPSS is the stop motors signal from the STOP MOTORS button on the user
interface modules through the upper and lower limit switches of the height movement system.
When not active, the signal applies +26 V to the coil of a relay (RL1) which, when energized,
switches the digital accelerator stop motors signal STMHOST to the LCU PCB as signal STMLTU.
On operation of a STOP MOTORS button or a height limit switch, RL1 de-energizes and STMHOST
disconnects from the LCU PCB.
Note: The other parts of the circuit to which signal STMPSS applies, and the related parts for signal
MPFUPS (the mains power supply failure indicator from the UPS), are not used in this application.

3.18.9 Operation and analysis of the radiation inhibit signal
Signal INHRADLCU is the radiation inhibit signal from from the LCU PCB. Operational amplifiers
(IC45 1/2) invert the signal and apply it to a comparator (IC40 8/9/14), which sends output signal
INHRADLTU to the digital accelerator. The output from this comparator also goes to a second
comparator (IC40 10/11/13), which operates the coil of a relay (RL2). When energized, the
contacts of the relay close, and the relay sends the radiation inhibit signal INHRADPWHOST2 to
the other equipment as signal INHRADPWHOST2.

3.19 Description and operation of the logic control unit PCB
3.19.1 Configuration and function of the logic control unit PCB
The LCU PCB is the primary control PCB of the treatment table. It has a field programmable logic
array (FPLA) and five LED displays on its front edge for the troubleshooting.
The LCU PCB receives digital and analog signals from the treatment table, the user interfaces
modules, the digital accelerator, and other equipment in the treatment table control system.
From these inputs, and with the control of the FPLA, the LCU PCB gives:
• Outputs that control switched functions such as brakes, clutches, and lamps
• Analog outputs that control motor speeds
• Logic outputs that send data to other parts of the control system.
The PCB has a number of circuits for these functions:
• On-board power supply and reference voltage generation
• Central processing
• Digital input signal processing
• Digital output signal processing
• Analog input signal processing
• Analog output signal processing
• LED display control.

3.19.2 Function of the power supply circuit of the LCU PCB
The LCU PCB is supplied with +15 V, -15 V and +5 V from the PSU via the backplane. These inputs
are distributed on power rails to the other devices on the LCU PCB.

The +15 V input is protected against transient spikes and overvoltages by diode D8, and is noise-
limited by an LC filter comprising inductor L1 and capacitors C42 and C36. The presence of the
+15 V supply is indicated by LED D2, which is illuminated when +15 V is present.
The -15 V input is protected by diode D1, smoothed by L2, C5 and C4, and indicated by LED D4.
The +5 V input is protected by diode D6, smoothed by L3, C35 and C34, and indicated by LED D2.
The LCU PCB produces four independent on-board reference voltage lines; three of +10 V and
one of -10 V.
Reference outputs VREF10P and VREFALCU are +10 V signals produced from the +15 V supply rail
by a reference voltage generator (IC29). The two reference outputs are buffered by low output
impedance operational amplifiers (IC26). Signal VREF10P is the reference voltage for the on-
board DACs.
Reference output VREF10N is a -10 V signal and VREFBLCU is a +10 V signal. They are produced
from the +15 V supply rail by reference voltage generator IC30 and buffered by amplifiers IC26.
Signal VREF10N is the reference voltage for the on-board ADCs
Two separate sources of 10 V reference are used to maintain system safety in the event of a 10 V
reference failure.

3.19.4 Description of the input signal processing
Digital input signal processing
There are 32 discrete digital inputs, and all of these signals are subjected to similar conditioning.
Each input is connected to a 10 kΩ pull-up resistor, and passes through an RC noise reduction
filter to a Schmidt-triggered inverter, which presents a clean, fast, digital signal to an input pin of
the FPLA.
For example, the active-low ENBXHOST input at PL1, pin A1 is the longitudinal movement enable
signal from the host machine. The 5 V logic input is filtered by R80, R74 and C88 and applied to
Schmitt-triggered inverter IC25 9/8 to become signal ENBXHOST. This signal is applied to the
INX_CONN pin of the FPLA.
Analog input signal processing
There are 40 analog inputs to the LCU PCB. The inputs comprise five sets of eight signals, of which
one input in each set is spare. Each set of inputs is assigned to a separate movement subsystem
and consists of the potentiometer, velocity readout and speed demand signals for the related
Precise Treatment Table™ Document ID: 45133701862 10
Corrective Maintenance Manual
©2019 Elekta Limited. Page 87

Product description
Description and operation of the logic control unit PCB
movement subsystem. All the analog signal inputs are in the range 0 V to +10 V. All the analog
signal inputs are conditioned in the same way.
Each signal is filtered against interference and noise by an RC filter of 100 kΩ resistors and 100 nF
capacitors. The line is protected against overvoltage and transient spikes by high-speed diodes
connected in reverse to the analog power supply rails (+15 V and 0 V). The filtered signal is then
buffered by a unity gain operational amplifier, which presents the incoming analog voltage to a
data acquisition system (DAS) without risk of transients. For example, longitudinal movement
velocity readout signal VFBXLTULCU from the LTU PCB is filtered by R126 and C141 while diode
D40 provides overvoltage and transient protection. The filtered signal is buffered by IC47 9/10/8
and applied as the AIN7 input to pin 2 of DAS IC46.

3.19.5 Description of the LED bar graph display
The LCU PCB has five different 10-bar LED displays attached to the front edge of the PCB. The
four top displays are assigned to the longitudinal, lateral, height and iso-rotation movement
subsystems and the lowest display is the same for all.
The LED displays are controlled by the FPLA, which outputs the display data on 10-bit bus
LED0:LED9. The bus signals are applied to five different 10-line, D-type, tri-state latches, which
are positive-edge triggered. The 10 outputs of each latch drives a separate LED display device.
The FPLA provides five separate clock outputs, one for each latch, and common output enable
signal OE. The assignments of the circuit are:
• The longitudinal movement display uses latch IC28 and LED display device D11 and is
clocked by signal CLKOPT4.
• The lateral movement display uses latch IC20 and LED display device D10 and is clocked
by signal CLKOPT3.
• The height movement display uses latch IC18 and LED display device D9 and is clocked
by signal CLKOPT2.
• The iso-rotation movement display uses latch IC17 and LED display device D7 and is
clocked by signal CLKOPT1.
• The common display uses latch IC14 and LED display device D5 and is clocked by signal
CLKOPT0. The LED display driver signals are listed in the PCB input and output section.
The LCU PCB has a parallel port which communicates with the FPLA for test and future
development of the PCB. This parallel port gives versatile input and output to the logical core of
the LCU. The description of this parallel port is not available in this manual.

Digital output signal processing
Document ID: 45133701862 10 Precise Treatment Table™

Product description
Description and operation of the logic control unit PCB
There are 24 discrete digital outputs from the FPLA. All of these signals are applied to line drivers
(IC1, IC4, IC10) that provide a versatile current capability and a high degree of spike and noise
protection. The outputs of the line drivers are simultaneously enabled by the OE signal from the
FPLA. Each discrete output line is pulled-up to +5 V.
The FPLA also outputs an 8-bit data bus (OPDB 7:0) and a 3-bit address bus (A2:0).
Analog output signal processing
The analog outputs from the LCU PCB are the velocity demand signals to the UMD PCBs of the
movement subsystems. The outputs comprise five output signals, one for each movement
subsystem.
All the analog signal outputs are produced in the same way. Digital data word OPDB7:OPDB0
from the FPLA is applied to a separate DAC for each movement subsystem. The DAC converts its
digital input into an analog voltage representing the required speed demand to the UMD PCB of
the respective movement subsystem.
The output of the DAC is scaled and signed by the value of VREF10N (-10 V), which produces a
DAC output of 0 V to 10 V. This voltage is buffered and reduced by 5 V by a unity gain operational
amplifier, to provide a -5 V to +5 V output. The devices assigned to each movement subsystem
are:
• The data word for the speed of longitudinal movement is handled by IC11 and
IC8 6/5/7.
• The data word for the speed of lateral movement is handled by IC6 and IC8 2/3/1.
• The data word for the speed of height movement is handled by IC3 and IC8 13/12/14.
• The data word for the speed of iso-rotation movement is handled by IC5 and
IC8 9/10/8.
• The data word for the speed of column rotation is handled by IC2 and IC7 9/10/8.
The analog output signals from the LCU PCB are listed in the PCB input and output section.

3.19.7 Description of the central processor and analog to digital signal
conversion
The central processor of the LCU PCB is the FPLA (IC15). You can think of the FPLA as a number of
functional blocks:
• Manual brake release control
• Motor enable
• Brake control
• Clutch control
• Interlocks.
The configuration data of the FPLA is programmed through the EPROM (IC49) which has
reprogrammable FLASH memory. Each time the system is powered-up, the configuration data is
loaded to the FPLA via the serial DATA0 line. The output of the EPROM is enabled by the STATUS
signal from the FPLA, the chip is selected by the CONFIG_DONE signal, and device timing is
controlled by the DCLK signal.
Precise Treatment Table™ Document ID: 45133701862 10
Corrective Maintenance Manual
©2019 Elekta Limited. Page 89

Product description
Description and operation of the universal motor drive PCBs
The system clock is obtained from a 3.686400 MHz crystal (X1) and Schmitt-triggered inverters
(IC12 and IC13). The FPLA is reset on timeout by a watchdog timer (IC16), which monitors the
WDI signal from the FPLA.
Analog to digital signal conversion
Each DAS is a single-chip device that consists of an 8-bit multiplexer, an 8-bit A DC, an 8 × 8-bit
dual-port RAM, and address latching and control logic. The reference voltage is signal VREF10N.
Analog to digital conversion is a continuous channel-sequencing process in which the 8 analog
inputs are converted into separate 8-bit data words. The result of each conversion is assigned to
a separate RAM address and read under the control of address latch enable signal ALE and 3-bit
address bus A2 to A0 from the FPLA. The data word read from the RAM is output via on-chip tri-
state buffers as data word IPDB7 to IPDN0, which is applied to the FPLA.
Each DAS also outputs an active-low STAT signal that is used to indicate the start of an internal
data conversion cycle. The STAT signals are applied to the FPLA.

3.20 Description and operation of the universal motor drive
PCBs
3.20.1 Configuration and function of the universal motor drive PCBs
There are five UMD PCBs in the EIM:
• I-UMD in the iso-rotation system
• X-UMD in the longitudinal movement system
• Y-UMD in the lateral movement system
• G1-UMD Ganty drive 1 (installed on digital accelerator Serial No. 152531 or later)
• G2-UMD Ganty drive 2 (installed on digital accelerator Serial No. 152531 or later).
The UMD PCBs send the necessary signals to operate the motors of the longitudinal movement,
lateral movement, iso-rotation, and gantry movement. All movements can use the same type of
UMD PCB, with no setup or adjustment necessary.
Each PCB receives a ±26 V power supply from the PSU, and each PCB has two input signals: a
demand signal and an enable signal. The UMD PCB gives an output signal as a result of the
demand signal only when the enable input signal is low (logic 0). A positive demand signal gives a
negative output signal.
There are two different modes of operation for the UMD PCBs:
• In the first mode, one UMD PCB drives the load from the ±26 V supply, and the return
from the load connects to 0 V. This is applicable to longitudinal, lateral and iso-rotation
movement.
• In the other mode, two UMD PCBs operate in a bridge to apply the drive to the two
sides of the load. In this mode, the demand input to one UMD PCB is inverted to give
the demand input to the other UMD PCB. This is applicable to gantry movement.
Document ID: 45133701862 10 Precise Treatment Table™
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 90 ©2019 Elekta Limited.

Product description
Description and operation of the universal motor drive PCBs
Servos in the movement systems sense the motor current and voltage, and use IR compensation,
to control the motor speed. To control the coil current, the servos sense only the current. The
servos also use an analysis of the back electromotive force (back EMF) to control the motor
speed. The servo senses the motor voltage and current, and subtracts the resistive or IR part.
Note: The test points at connector TPU1 on each UMD PCB are used during PCB manufacture. You will
not use them during corrective maintenance.
