Product description
Description and operation of the universal motor drive PCBs
Figure 3.50 Circuits on the universal motor drive PCBs
In the voltage regulator circuit, the internal ±15 V power supplies for the UMD PCB come from
the ±26 V power supplies, through standard 1 A regulators (IC4 and IC5). Fusible resistors (R1 and
R2) prevent damage to the PCB if a fault occurs, and decrease dissipation in the regulators.
In the current sensor circuit, the shunt resistors (R50 to R53) sense the output current. Resistor
R53 is always in the circuit with the load. Resistors R50 to R52 can be in or out of the cicuit. The
maximum necessary output current controls the links (in or out of the circuit) for resistors R50 to
R52 (the links are part of the rack wiring, and are therefore external to the UMD PCB). A
differential amplifier (IC1B) with high common mode rejection ratio (CMRR), and IC1C, increases
the shunt resistor voltage by a factor of 6.8. This gives a voltage at PL1, pin a14 in proportion to
the sensed current.
In the current limit circuit, Zener diodes (D5 and D6) control the output from the current sensor
circuit when the output becomes ±7.5 V (6.8 V + 0.7 V). This decreases the demand signal to IC1D
Document ID: 45133701862 10 Precise Treatment Table™
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 92 ©2019 Elekta Limited.

Product description
Description and operation of the universal motor drive PCBs
and applies a limit to the output current. When this occurs, the sense voltage across resistors R50
to R53 is 1.1 V.
In the IR compensation circuit, the current sensor output for the motor drives goes to one of the
IR compensation inputs (IR COMP 1 to IR COMP 5), which subtracts the current sensor output
from a sample of the output voltage in amplifier IC1A. The output of the amplifier is a voltage
that is in proportion to the speed of the motor. The circuit also uses this voltage to adjust for wire
and armature resistance.
In the error amplifier circuit, the DEMAND input (PL1, pin a9), with a range of ±5.0 V, adds to the
FEEDBACK at pin c9 or c10 to supply the gain necessary. The error amplifier (IC1D) increases the
result of the added signals and sends an output to a pulse-width modulator.
In the pulse-width modulator circuit, a 110 kHz triangular wave generator (IC2D and IC3B) sends
its output of approximately ±6.0 V (peak) to comparators IC2A and IC2B. Diodes D7 and D8 apply
a 3 V level shift, which gives a 1 µs deadband to the back and front edges of the bipolar
modulator output. Diodes D3 and D4 limit the output voltage of IC1D to give approximately 11 V.
The R21 and C16 phase-shift network make the circuit more stable.
In the output stages circuit, the positive output from the modulator switches on TR4. This
switches on the parallel p-channel MOSFETs (TR1 and TR2) through diode D10. To switch on TR1
and TR2 fully, the potential divider (R35 and R36, and R37 to R39) makes the gate (in the
MOSFET) go 10 V below the source. An emitter follower (TR3) discharges the gate-source
capacitance of the MOSFETs to switch them off. The typical time to switch on or off is 100 ns. A
configuration of the p-channel (D11 and R42 to R46) switches on and switches off the n-channel
MOSFETs (TR7 and TR8). Flyback diodes D12 and D13 limit the output to L1 during the deadband
period. An LC filter circuit (L1 and C20 to C22) with a threshold frequency of approximately 10
kHz controls the output to decrease unwanted, remaining, small changes in the output signal.
In the enable circuit, the ENABLE input signal at PL1 (pin c12) is active low. The input signal goes
to a comparator (IC2C) through a potential divider circuit (R31 to R33). In the disabled state, the
comparator gives a high output (pulled up by R34). The JFETs (TR9 and TR11) switch on to stop
the signal to the error amplifier and the output stage. When the ENABLE input is less than +5 V,
the output of the comparator (IC2C) changes to -15 V. This switches off the JFETs (TR9 and TR11)
to let the circuit operate.
In the power failure protection circuit, a comparator (IC2C) gives a high output that switches on
the JFETs (TR9 and TR11) to disable the UMD PCB if the -15 V supply fails. If the +15 V supply fails,
the gate to JFET TR10 goes to -15 V and TR10 switches on (fully). This stops the signal to the
output stage.
In the inverter circuit, when two UMD PCBs operate in a bridge, the input signals go to a unity
gain inverter (IC3C). The demand signal INVERTER INPUT goes to PL1 (pin a13) and the output
signal INVERTER OUTPUT comes from PL1 (pin c13). During bridge mode operation, the INVERTER
OUTPUT from one UMD PCB goes to the DEMAND input of the other UMD PCB of the pair.
3.20.3 Signal and power supply to the emergency down drive module
The EDD module lets you lower the treatment table to a safe position if a mains power supply
failure occurs while the UPS supplies power. The EDD module is not installed on digital
accelerators Serial No. 152531 and later. For systems 152531 and later, the UPS will shutdown
after approximately 90 seconds after a controlled shutdown. If there is a mains power supply
failure, the UPS will continue to supply power until the battery is discharged.
The 24 V AC interlock supply signal 24VACHOST goes from the host machine, through the
backplane, to the EDD module. When the mains electrical supply is present, the 24VACHOST
signal goes, through the (Normally Closed) contacts 21/22 of relay RL1, to relay RL1 on the LED
PCB within the PSU. Relay RL1 on the LED PCB determines which interlocks to use (that is, host or
local).
A nominal +26 V DC supply signal STMPSS from pin 3 of SK801U connects to coil connection A1 of
relay RL1. During mains power failure, internal batteries keep the output of the UPS stable, and
Precise Treatment Table™ Document ID: 45133701862 10
Corrective Maintenance Manual

Product description
Description and operation of the universal motor drive PCBs
therefore signal STMPSS stays at +26 V, until the UPS shuts down. When the UPS detects the
mains power failure, it sends signal MPFUPS, through terminal A2, to energize RL1. This signal
tells the firmware in the LCU PCB to start emergency down drive mode. Contacts 21/22 of RL1
then open to remove the 24 V AC supply signal 24VACHOST(OUT) to the LED PCB, and the
interlocks change from host to local.
Signal 24VACHOST then goes to RL2, through the Normally Open contacts 11/14 of RL1, to start a
nominal 5 minute timer. When the timer stops, normally open contacts 9/5 of RL2 close. And the
level translated (nominal 14 V) signal HSHUTDOWN(OUT)goes to the shut-down input of the UPS.
The UPS completes its shut-down cycle, after which, the EDD is not available.
If signal 24VACHOST is not present (the mains electrical supply to the host machine has failed),
there is no power to timer relay RL2 and there will be no signal to the UPS to shut down. The UPS
will continue to supply electrical power until there is no more power from its internal batteries.
During this time, the EDD will be available.
A break in the interlock chain of the host machine, for example through the operation of the
emergency off switch, on the wall of the treatment room, removes the mains supply to the UPS
and stops the 24VACHOST signal. This puts the system into EDD mode during the Closed machine
state and FACILITY 1 inhibits. If you operate the ENABLE bar when the EDD is active, the height
motor brake releases, and the treatment table slowly lowers. No other motorized movements
are permitted while the EDD is active.

3.20.4 Configuration and function of the inline calibration PCB
The inline calibration PCB has a number of amplifiers that change the levels of some output

signals supply the necessary ±90 V power for the operation of the height motors.

For service and test procedures, you can configure the treatment table to have no connection to
the digital linear accelerator. This configuration is referred to as stand-alone mode.

3.21 Description and operation of the movement servo loops
3.21.1 Movement servo loops
In Integrity™ program, movement servo loops control the movement of each movement
subsystem.
The servo has a primary and secondary encoder that supply primary and secondary position
readouts to the digital accelerator.
• The primary encoder has two potentiometers (coarse and fine) which supply the
position information at different resolutions.
• The secondary encoder has a check potentiometer that monitors the operation of the
other two potentiometers.
Each potentiometer is directly connected to the movement assembly. The voltages read from the
potentiometers are in proportion to the actual position of the movement assembly. The voltages
are digitized and sent to the digital accelerator as servo feedback signals.

3.21.2 Movement encoders
The primary encoder contains the fine and coarse potentiometers. The primary encoder supplies
the position information to the digital accelerator. The digital accelerator uses the data to
accurately calculate the movement position.
The secondary encoder contains the check potentiometer. The secondary encoder supplies data
from the check potentiometer to the digital accelerator. This lets the digital accelerator monitor
the primary encoder data to make sure that it is correct.

3.21.3 Movement encoders - coarse potentiometer
The coarse potentiometer has one wiper. The coarse potentiometer drive gear rotates the wiper
approximately 10 turns during the full range of movement in one direction.
The digital accelerator uses the coarse potentiometer outputs to identify the number of turns
that the fine potentiometer completes. The coarse potentiometer output lets the control system
select wiper A or wiper B of the fine potentiometer as the data source it will use.
The voltage curves of the check and coarse potentiometers are straight lines in the approximate
range 0 V to 10 V. The position of the potentiometer on the related drive shaft has an effect on
the actual value of the output. Usually there is a small offset between the output values of the
fine and coarse potentiometers. This offset must be kept to a minimum during setup.
3.21.4 Movement encoders - fine potentiometer
The fine potentiometer measures 0 V to 10 V approximately 20 times across the complete range
of movement. This gives a sawtooth voltage waveform. The fine potentiometer has no end stops,
therefore, continuous rotation is possible. See Figure 3.51

3.21.5 Movement encoders - check potentiometer
The check potentiometer has one wiper. The check potentiometer drive gear rotates the wiper
approximately 10 turns during the full range of the movement in one direction.
The check potentiometer is part of the secondary encoder, and its drive gear is not part of the
primary encoder. Therefore, if a malfunction causes a drive gear fault, a difference will occur
between the primary and secondary encoders.
The check potentiometer also has a dedicated signal path and reference voltage that is not part
of the signal paths for the fine and coarse potentiometers. If an electronic fault occurs, the
difference between the primary and secondary encoder signals will cause an error.
3.21.6 Movement encoders - positional resolution
The coarse potentiometer gives a low resolution indication of the movement position. For
example, in the isocentric rotation readout, the coarse potentiometer gives the actual value of
the isocentric angle at a resolution of approximately 36°/V. The coarse potentiometer also points
to one of the curves of the fine potentiometer, which gives the angle at a resolution of 1.8°/V.
Therefore, the readout circuit can show the isocentric rotation angle to two decimal places.
3.21.7 Movement encoders - potentiometer gearing
In the height movement subsystem, there is a ratio between the gearing of the fine and coarse
potentiometers. As the height carriage assembly moves through its full vertical range of
movement in one direction, the fine potentiometer will rotates approximately 20 times. The
output range of the fine potentiometer is 200 V, (20 × 10 V). This multiplies the total track length
by approximately 20, therefore, the readout and signal-to-noise ratio indications have an
accuracy of ±0.05%.
In the other movement subsystems, longitudinal, lateral, iso-rotation, and column rotation, there
is also a ratio between the gearing of the fine and coarse potentiometers. In each of these
movement subsystems:
• The fine potentiometer rotates 10 times during the full range of movement in one
direction.
• The readout and signal-to-noise ratio indications have an accuracy of ±0.1%.
3.21.8 Correlating the readout potentiometers
The control software of the digital accelerator must know the relation between the three
potentiometer curves to calculate the position of a movement. During the correlation procedure,
the table movements are made in Learn mode. Learn mode calculates the slope and intersection
point of each potentiometer curve.
The potentiometer tracks are not fully linear tracks. Therefore, before calibration, use Learn
mode to calculate the correlation coefficients of the potentiometers. The system saves the
correlation coefficients while the movement subsystem moves between its end points. The
correlation coefficients are only applicable to one set of potentiometers. If a new potentiometer
is installed, you must do the potentiometer correlation again.
Precise Treatment Table™ Document ID: 45133701862 10

Product description
Description and operation of the movement servo loops
After the Learn mode procedures, the software knows the correct fine potentiometer curve to
use for a coarse potentiometer voltage. The software also knows how the output voltage of the
check potentiometer is related to the output voltage of the coarse potentiometer.
For each movement subsystem, the digital accelerator keeps three performance indicators,
which measure:
• The potentiometer correlation errors of the fine potentiometer.
• The phase errors of the fine potentiometer in relation to the coarse potentiometer.
• The errors between the check potentiometer and the coarse potentiometer.
Note: Part 156 of the check potentiometer item of a movement subsystem shows the potentiometer
correlation errors for that movement subsystem. Part 156 shows the maximum difference
between the coarse and check potentiometers.
3.21.9 Description of the positional calibration of a three potentiometer
circuit
When calibration of the three potentiometer circuit is necessary, the same calibration procedure
is applicable for each movement subsystem. There are two reference points for the movement
calibrations.
To prepare for the calibration procedure you must set the:
• Related software item to manual control
• Applicable action mask value
• Digital accelerator to the Preparatory machine state.
You can use Learn mode to calibrate the potentiometer correlation.
When you set the software item to manual control, it will:
• Decrease the movement speed
• Override the software limits
• Ignore the position errors.
The applicable action mask value overrides the potentiometer correlation errors. The
Preparatory machine state makes movements of the treatment table possible.

3.22 Description of the longitudinal movement control for the
treatment table system
3.22.1 Longitudinal movement control
The buttons and thumbwheels on user interface modules control the movement of the
treatment table along the longitudinal axis. A user interface module is installed at each side of
the treatment table. There are two types of user interface module for treatment tables with, and
without, independent longitudinal and lateral brakes.
The treatment tables without independent longitudinal and lateral brakes have a LAT/LONG
BRAKE button. The treatment tables with independent longitudinal and lateral brakes have a
LONGITUDINAL brake button.
To move the treatment table, press and hold the ENABLE bar, and operate the LONGITUDINAL
thumbwheel, to move the treatment table. The treatment table moves in the direction that you
operate the thumbwheel. The thumbwheel also controls the speed at which the treatment table
moves.
When it is necessary, you can move the treatment table manually. But, make sure that you
release the brake first. Apply the brake after you complete the treatment table movements.

3.22.2 Longitudinal motor drive circuit
When the LONGITUDINAL thumbwheel of a user interface module is moved, the speed and
direction of movement are represented by the VELXPSS demand signal, which is an analog
voltage of 4.3 V to 5.7 V, centered on +5.0 V. The signal is applied to the LTU PCB, where it is
translated to a level of 0 V to +10 V. The voltage is then applied to the LCU PCB as signal
VELXPSSLTU. Another version of the VELXPSS signal, in the range 3.6 V to 6.4 V DC, is applied to
the digital accelerator as signal VELXPSSBUF.
The LTU PCB also receives signal VELXHOS, a longitudinal movement velocity demand signal in
the range -5 V to +5 V from the digital accelerator. The signal VELXHOST2 in the range -12 V to
+12 V is an alternative input from other equipment. On the LTU PCB, the velocity demand signals
are level translated and one of the demands is applied to the LCU PCB as signal VELXLTU in the
range 0 V to +10 V, centered on +5.0 V.
On the LCU PCB, the VELXPSSLTU or VELXLTU signal is converted into an 8-bit data word that
represents the demanded speed and direction of movement. The data bus is applied to the FPLA,
which is programmed to respond by issuing a motor speed and direction command in the form of
an 8-bit output data word. The output data word is then converted into analog drive signal
VELXLCU in the range -5 V to +5 V. The drive signal is applied to the X-UMD PCB.
When enabled by signal ENBXLTUXMD from the LTU PCB, the X-UMD PCB outputs signal MOTX to
the motor of the longitudinal movement subsystem. MOTX is a 0 V to ±26 V DC signal that drives
the motor at the commanded speed and direction.
Precise Treatment Table™ Document ID: 45133701862 10
Corrective Maintenance Manual
©2019 Elekta Limited. Page 101

3.22.3 Longitudinal movement enable circuit
The movement ENABLE bar of the user interface module is represented by active-low signal
ENBPSS, which is applied to the LTU PCB. The signal is logic 0 while the ENABLE bar is held
pressed. On the LTU PCB, the ENBPSS signal is subjected to logic gating with other interlock
signals. The movement enable output of the LTU PCB is signal ENBXLTU to the LCU PCB. If all
interlock conditions are satisfied, the ENBXLTU signal is logic 0.
On the LCU PCB, the ENBXLTU signal is applied as a single input to the FPLA. Longitudinal
movement enable signal ENBXHOST from the digital accelerator is also input to the same pin of
the FPLA. In one of the two motor enable inputs is received, the FPLA produces +26 V enable
signal ENBXLCU to the LTU PCB.
On the LTU PCB, the +26 V level of signal ENBXLCU is translated into 0 V to +15 V logic signal
EMBXLTUXMD, which gives the motor drive enable signal to the X-UMD PCB.

3.22.4 Longitudinal movement brake control circuit
When the longitudinal movement brake is released, the FPLA on the LCU PCB sends an active-low
brake release command BRKXLCU to the LTU PCB. On the LTU PCB, the signal is translated into
the power signal BRKXLTU, which applies +52 V, then +26 V, across the coil of the longitudinal
movement brake. The action of the coil overcomes the force of the mechanical spring that holds
the brake on.
When the longitudinal movement brake is applied, the FPLA removes the brake release
command BRKXLCU to logic 1, and therefore the power supply to the coil of the brake is
removed.

3.22.5 Longitudinal movement brake release lamp control circuit
While the longitudinal movement brake is on, the FPLA on the LCU PCB turns off the active-low
brake release indicator signal MBIXYLCU to the LTU PCB. On the LTU PCB, drive signal MBIXYLCU
is sent by a transistor switch, to the lamp in the longitudinal brake button on the user interface
modules.
When the longitudinal movement brake is released, the FPLA on the LCU PCB sets signal
MBIXYLCU and, on the LTU PCB, drive signal MBIXYLCU is removed from the lamp in the
longitudinal brake button on the user interface modules.
3.22.6 Longitudinal movement clutch control circuit
When longitudinal movement is enabled, the FPLA on the LCU PCB gives the active-low brake
command CLUXLCU on the LTU PCB. On the LTU PCB, the signal is translated into power signal
CLUXLTU, which applies +26 V and 0 V across the clutch of the longitudinal motor.

3.22.7 Longitudinal movement positional readout circuits
The three potentiometers of the longitudinal movement subsystem are each supplied with +10 V
and 0 V reference signals. The levels of the signals from the wipers of the potentiometers
represent the actual longitudinal position of the treatment table. These signals give positional
readback data in the range 0 V to +10 V, centered on +5 V.
The signals are:
• CRSXPSS is the voltage from the wiper of the longitudinal movement coarse
potentiometer.
• FNAXPSS is the voltage from wiper A of the longitudinal movement fine potentiometer.
• FNBXPSS is the voltage from wiper B of the longitudinal movement fine potentiometer.
• CHKXPSS is the voltage from the wiper of the longitudinal movement check
potentiometer.
Signal CRSXPSS is sent to the LTU PCB, where the signal is level-translated to become output
CRSXLTU to the digital accelerator and used to display the longitudinal position of the treatment
table.
The LTU PCB also converts the CRSXPSS signal to a voltage that is proportional to the speed of the
longitudinal movement. This gives a unipolar velocity feedback signal VFBXLTULCU to the
LCU PCB.
On the LCU PCB, the four readback signals and velocity signal VFBXLTULCU are converted into 8-
bit data words that give the position of the treatment table on the longitudinal axis and the
speed of movement. The data is applied to the FPLA on the LCU PCB, which uses the information
to modify drive signal VELXLCU to the X-UMD PCB.

3.23 Description of the lateral movement control for the
treatment table system
3.23.1 Lateral movement control
The lateral movement of the treatment table is controlled from one of the user interface
modules. This is the same as the control of the longitudinal movement, and uses the LAT/LONG
BRAKE button, the movement ENABLE bar and the LATERAL thumbwheel.

3.23.2 Lateral motor drive circuit
The lateral motor drive circuit operates the same as the longitudinal circuit. Demand signal
VELYPSS represents the speed and direction of movement, which is translated by the LTU PCB
into signal VELYPSSLTU to the LCU PCB and signal VELYPSSBUF to the digital accelerator. The
LTU PCB receives signals VELYHOST1 and VELYHOST2, one of which is output as signal VELYLTU to
the LCU PCB.
On the LCU PCB, the analog velocity demand is digitized and processed by the FPLA to generate
an 8-bit speed and direction command that is converted into analog drive signal VELYLCU to the
Y-UMD PCB.
When enabled by signal ENBYLTUYMD from the LTU PCB, the Y-UMD PCB outputs drive signal
MOTY to the motor of the lateral movement subsystem.
3.23.3 Lateral movement enable circuit
The lateral movement enable drive circuit operates the same as the longitudinal circuit. Enable
signal ENBPSS is applied to the LTU PCB, where the signal is gated with other interlock signals to
send the ENBYLTU signal to the LCU PCB.
On the LCU PCB, the FPLA uses the ENBYLTU signal, or the ENBZHOST signal from the digital
accelerator, to generate the enable signal ENBYLCU to the LTU PCB. This signal is then translated
into logic signal ENBYLTUYMD, which is the motor drive enable signal to the Y-UMD PCB.
3.23.4 Lateral movement brake control circuit
The lateral movement brake control circuit operates the same as the longitudinal circuit. When
the lateral movement brake is released, the FPLA sends signal BRKYLCU to the LTU PCB, where
the signal is translated into power output BRKYLTU to the coil of the lateral movement brake.
When the lateral movement brake is applied, the FPLA removes the brake release command
BRKYLCU to logic 1, and therefore the power supply to the coil of the brake is removed.

switch, to the lamp in the LATERAL BRAKE button. When the lateral
movement brake is released, the FPLA on the LCU PCB sets signal MBISLCU
and, on the LTU PCB, drive signal MBISLCU is removed from the LATERAL
BRAKE button.

3.24 Description of the height movement control for the
treatment table system
3.24.1 Height movement control
The height movement of the treatment table is controlled from one of the user interface
modules, and uses the movement ENABLE bar and the HEIGHT thumbwheel.
This section gives information on the control height movement circuits. This is the only
movement subsystem to include limit switches.
Related Links:
Height movement positional readout circuits on page 107
Height movement brake control circuit on page 107
Height motor drive circuit on page 106
Height movement enable circuit on page 106
3.24.2 Height motor drive circuit
The height motor drive circuit operates the same as the longitudinal and lateral circuits, apart
from the ZMD unit. The ZMD supplies the motor drive power, which is an alternative to the
UMD PCB.
Demand signal VELZPSS shows the speed and direction of movement. The LTU PCB changes this
demand signal into signal VELZPSSLTU to the LCU PCB and VELZPSSBUF to the digital accelerator.
The LTU PCB also receives signals VELZHOST1 and VELZHOST2, one of which is output as signal
VELZLTU to the LCU PCB.
The LCU PCB digitizes the analog velocity demand signal. The FPLA sends this demand signal to
make an 8-bit speed and direction command that changes into an analog drive signal VELZLCU to
the ZMD unit.
The LTU PCB sends signal ENBZLTUZMD to enable the ZMD unit. The ZMD unit then outputs drive
signals MOTZ1 and MOTZ2 to the motor of the height movement subsystem.
3.24.3 Height movement enable circuit
The height movement enable drive circuit operates the same as the longitudinal and lateral
circuits. Enable signal ENBPSS is applied to the LTU PCB, where the signal is gated with other
interlock signals to send the ENBZLTU signal to the LCU PCB.
On the LCU PCB, the FPLA sends the ENBZLTU signal, or the ENBZHOST signal from the digital
accelerator, to give an enable signal ENBZLCU to the LTU PCB. This signal is then translated into
logic signal EMBZLTUZMD, which is the motor drive enable signal to the ZMD unit.
Document ID: 45133701862 10 Precise Treatment Table™

3.24.4 Height movement brake control circuit
The height movement brake control circuit is the same as the longitudinal and lateral circuits.
When the height movement brake is released, the FPLA sends signal BRKZLCU to the LTU PCB.
The LTU PCB changes the active low signal into the power output BRKZLTU to the coil of the
height movement brake.
3.24.5 Height movement positional readout circuits
The operation of the positional readout circuit for height movement is equivalent to the
longitudinal and lateral circuits.
The potentiometer outputs used by the height circuit are:
• CRSZPSS
• FNAZPSS
• FNBZPSS
• CHKZPSS
Signal CRZYPSS is level-translated by the LTU PCB to become output CRSZLTU to the digital
accelerator, which uses the signal to display the height of the treatment table. The CRSZPSS
signal changes into a velocity feedback signal VFBZLTULCU to the LCU PCB.
On the LCU PCB, the four readback signals and velocity signal change into data words that give
the position and speed of vertical movement of the treatment table. The data is applied to the
FPLA on the LCU PCB, which uses the information to modify drive signal VELZLCU to the ZMD
unit.
3.25 Description of the iso-rotation control system
3.25.1 Iso-rotation control
The controls on the user interface modules control the iso-rotation of the treatment table. This is
the same as the control of the longitudinal and lateral movements, and uses the ISO-ROTATION
BRAKE button, the ENABLE bar, and the ISO-ROTATION thumbwheel to move the treatment
table.

3.25.2 Iso-rotation motor drive circuit
The iso-rotation motor drive circuit operates the same as the longitudinal and lateral circuits.
Demand signal VELIPSS shows the speed and direction of movement, which is translated by the
LTU PCB into signal VELIPSSLTU to the LCU PCB and VELIPSSBUF to the digital accelerator. The

On the LCU PCB, the analog velocity demand is digitized and sent by the FPLA to make an 8-bit

When enabled by signal ENBILTUIMD from the LTU PCB, the I-UMD PCB outputs drive signal

3.25.3 Iso-rotation movement enable circuit
The iso-rotation movement enable drive circuit operates the same as the longitudinal and lateral

accelerator, to generate the enable signal ENBILCU to the LTU PCB. This signal is then translated
into logic signal EMBILTUIMD, which is the motor drive enable signal to the I-UMD PCB.
3.25.4 Iso-rotation brake control circuit
The iso-rotation brake control circuit operates the same way as the longitudinal, lateral and
height circuits. When the ISO-ROTATION BRAKE is released, the FPLA sends signal BRKILCU to the
LTU PCB, where the signal is translated into power output BRKILTU to the coil of the iso-rotation
brake.
3.25.5 Iso-rotation clutch control circuit
The iso-rotation clutch control circuit operates the same way as the longitudinal and lateral
circuits. When the iso- rotation clutch control circuit is enabled, the FPLA sends signal CLUILCU to
the LTU PCB, where the signal is translated into power output CLUILTU to the clutch of the iso-
rotation motor.

Description of the column rotation control system
Signal CRSIPSS is level-translated by the LTU PCB to become output CRSILTU to the digital
accelerator, which uses the signal is used to display the iso-rotation angle of the treatment. The
CRSIPSS signal is also converted into velocity feedback signal VFBILTULCU to the LCU PCB.

3.26 Description of the column rotation control system
3.26.1 Column rotation control
The column of the treatment table is rotated manually, therefore the column rotation movement
subsystem does not include a drive motor and clutch or related control circuits. But, the
subsystem does include a brake and positional readback potentiometers.
Related Links:
Column rotation brake control circuit on page 109
Column rotation positional readout circuits on page 109
3.26.2 Column rotation brake control circuit
The column rotation brake control circuit operates the same as the longitudinal, lateral, height
and iso-rotation circuits. When the brake is released, the FPLA sends signal BRKCLCU to the
LTU PCB, where the signal is translated into power output BRKCLTU to the coil of the column
rotation brake.
3.26.3 Column rotation positional readout circuits
The positional readout circuit for column rotation operates the longitudinal, lateral, height and
iso-rotation circuits, but the readouts are not used to give a motor drive signal. The
potentiometer outputs used by the column rotation circuit are:
• CRSCPSS
• FNACPSS
• FNBCPSS
• CHKCPSS.
Signal CRSCPSS is level-translated by the LTU PCB to become output CRSCLTU to the digital
accelerator, which uses the signal to display the column rotation angle of the treatment table.
3.27 Description of the interlock signal control system
3.27.1 Stop motors and height movement limit switch control circuit
The STOP MOTORS buttons on the two user interface modules are connected in series with the
+26V IL power rail.
Precise Treatment Table™ Document ID: 45133701862 10
Corrective Maintenance Manual
©2019 Elekta Limited. Page 109

Product description
Description of the interlock signal control system
The output line from the buttons, signal STMLK, is then connected in series with the upper limit
switches (SW825A and SW825B) and lower limit switches (SW835A and SW835B) of the height
movement subsystem. The output line from the limit switches and signal STMPSS, is applied to
the LTU PCB.
On the LTU PCB, the STMPSS signal is applied to the coil of relay RL1, the contacts of which are
held closed by the +26 V level of signal STMPSS. In this energized condition, the contacts of RL1
switch stop motors signal STMHOST from the digital accelerator to the LCU PCB as signal
STMLTU.
If a STOP MOTORS button is pressed, or if a height limit switch operates, the +26 V rail is
disconnected and signal STMPSS is therefore asserted (0 V). When this occurs, RL1 is de-
energized and stop motors signal STMHOST is disconnected.
Related Links:
Function of the interlock circuits in the LED PCB on page 63
3.27.2 Touchguard override control circuit
The TOUCHGUARD OVERRIDE buttons on the two user interface modules are connected in series
with touchguard override signal TGOHOST from the digital accelerator.
The contacts of the TOUCHGUARD OVERRIDE buttons are usually open, therefore the procedure
for one of the two buttons causes an interlock when you apply the TGOHOST signal to the
LTU PCB. The signal is also sent back to the digital accelerator as signal TGOPSS.
On the LTU PCB, the TGOPSS signal is gated with other interlock signals to cause an composite
velocity inhibit signal VELINHLTU, which is applied to the LCU PCB. The TGOPSS signal also
supplies the output TGOLTU to the LCU PCB.
On the LCU PCB, the TGOLTU signal is applied as a discrete input to the FPLA. The FPLA gives an
active-low indicator signal TGILCU to the LTU PCB. The LTU PCB uses the digital input TGILCU to
give 0 V to 26 V current drive signal TGILTU. This illuminates the lamp in the active TOUCHGUARD
OVERRIDE button on the user interface module.
Related Links:
Function of the interlock circuits in the LED PCB on page 63
3.27.3 Touchguard flag control circuit
Signal TGFHOST1 from the movements interlock circuits of the digital accelerator and related
signal TGFHOST2 from other equipment, are applied to the LTU PCB. The signals are gated and
level-translated to supply active-low signal TGFLTU to the LCU PCB. On the LCU PCB, the
TGFLTUsignal is applied as a discrete input to the interlock function of the FPLA.
Related Links:
Function of the interlock circuits in the LED PCB on page 63
3.27.4 HT interlock control circuit
HT interlock signal HTINTHOST derives from the secondary HT interlock chain of the digital
accelerator and is asserted (high) when an interlock condition shown. The signal is applied to the
LTU PCB, where it is level-translated into active-high signal HTINTLTU to the LCU PCB. On the
LCU PCB, the HTINTLTU signal is applied as a discrete input to the interlock function of the FPLA.

3.28 Description of the manual brake release indication control
3.28.1 Manual brake release indication control
The lamps in the brake buttons of the user interface modules give an indication when you apply
or release the manual brakes. Control of the manual brake release indication circuit is different if
the treatment table has longitudinal/lateral brakes that operate independently.
Related Links:

3.28.2 Manual brake release indication control without independently
operated longitudinal and lateral brakes
The brake release buttons on each user interface module are connected in series, and the brake
signal lines from the two user interface modules are connected in parallel. When a brake button
is pressed to release the related brake, it sends the SWDPSS analog signal (in the range 0 V to
5.2 V) to the LCU PCB.
The EPROM on the LTU PCB decodes the SWDPSS signal and sends the brake release commands
MBKCLTU, MBKILTU, and MBKXYLTU to the LCU PCB.
On the LCU PCB, the brake release commands are applied as discrete inputs to the FPLA. When
the FPLA receives the brake release command, it sends a related active-low indicator signal to the
LTU PCB.
The LTU PCB changes the digital input to a 0 V to 26 V current drive signal. This causes the lamp
in the active brake button on the user interface module to come on. The outputs of the LCU PCB
are:

• MBICLCU is the manual brake release indicator for column rotation.

3.28.3 Manual brake release indication control for independently operated
longitudinal and lateral brakes
There is a difference in the brake release indicator circuit for systems with, and without,
independently operated longitudinal and lateral brakes. In systems with independently operated
brakes, each brake has a dedicated control and indication circuit which operates each brake
independently.
In systems without independently operated brakes, one control and indication circuit operates
the longitudinal and lateral brakes at the same time. The circuit operates the same as the
longitudinal brake of the independently operated brake system. The control and indication circuit
of the lateral brake is not used.
The LTU PCB decodes the brake release signal SWDPSS and sends the fourth brake release output
RELISOLTU to the LCU PCB. The LCU PCB applies the lateral brake release command to the FPLA,
which sends a related active-low indicator signal MBISLTU to the LTU PCB. The LTU PCB changes
the digital input to the current drive signal MBISLTU. This causes the lamp in the LATERAL brake
button on the user interface module to come on.
Related Links:
Manual brake release indication control without independently operated longitudinal and
lateral brakes on page 111

3.29.2 Treatment table software parts
Each software item has many different parameters known as parts. These parts do different
functions, for example, change data values to actual units, voltage units, amps, degrees, and
millimeters.
In the control items assigned to the treatment table, there is a servo item for each movement
subsystem:
• i156 iso-rotational movement servo item
• i157 longitudinal movement servo item
• i158 lateral movement servo item
• i159 height movement servo item.
In each servo item, there are two parts that have an effect on the performance of the position
servo:
Precise Treatment Table™ Document ID: 45133701862 10
Corrective Maintenance Manual
©2019 Elekta Limited. Page 113

Product description
Description and operation of the software control for the treatment table system
• Part 132 - slow down point, at which speed is limited and deceleration occurs.
• Part 141 - position gain, which determines the accuracy of the positioning.
There are also three parts that have an effect on the performance when small movements are
made:
• Part 135 - threshold, at which ASU correction starts.
• Part 142 - ASU increment gain, the increment value of each ASU correction.
• Part 140 - deadband, the positional error value at which ASU correction starts.
Slow down point
Part 132 sets a distance value to the last position of the movement. At the set distance, the
maximum speed is decreased to make sure that the system decelerates. If the value of Part 132 is
increased, deceleration occurs earlier. Because of IEC requirements, the value of Part 132 must
not be decreased below the default value.
Position gain
Part 141 sets the gain of the servo and therefore determines the accuracy of the positioning. If
the value of Part 141 is too high, the movement tends to oscillate about the required positional
setting, so the value of Part 141 should be such that it does not oscillate or overshoot by an
unacceptable amount.
If the movement undershoots, the value of Part 141 should be increased. If the movement
overshoots or oscillates, the value of Part 141 should be reduced. The performance may be
different in the two directions, so it may be necessary to compromise between undershoot in
one direction and overshoot in the other.
Part 132 and Part 141 can both be used to prevent oscillation, but their effects are subtly
different. Part 141 has most effect close to the final position because, for larger errors, the speed
is limited by the speed limits or by the slow down position. In general, the value of Part 141
should be reduced only if it is not possible to prevent overshoot/oscillation by reasonable
adjustment of Part 132.
It may be advisable to disable the nudging facility while determining the optimum values for Part
132 and Part 141.
Threshold
Part 135 applies a starting correction so that the ASU correction does not start from zero, thus
reducing the time taken to reach the correction that causes movement. Part 135 has a range of 0
to 50.
ASU increment gain
For small movements, the positional correction may not provide sufficient drive to allow the
motor to overcome stiction. If no movement is detected, an ASU correction (process_param_7 of
the servo item) is applied. The ASU correction is incremented by Part 142 at each occurrence of
the host machine control system loop (every 120 ms), until movement occurs when the ASU
correction is reset to zero.
The ASU correction applies a series of nudges which move the treatment table to the desired
position. Since the amount of correction reduces as the movement nears the desired position,
the amplitude of the ASU correction to cause movement increases. The ASU correction thus
forms a sawtooth waveform of increasing amplitude.
Part 142 has a range of 0 to 5.
Deadband
ASU correction is not applied unless the positional error is greater than the value of Part 140.
This can be used to prevent small oscillations about the desired position, but will reduce the
accuracy of positioning. Part 140 has a range of 0 to 10. If the ASU_correction is plotted (using
the graphing facility) against time while holding down the PSS ASU buttons for about 10 to
Document ID: 45133701862 10 Precise Treatment Table™
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 114 ©2019 Elekta Limited.

Product description
Description and operation of the software control for the treatment table system
15 seconds, the resulting sawtooth will have in the order of 1 to 5 peaks with increasing
amplitude.
3.29.3 Description of the XVI table factors
If the treatment table is installed on a digital accelerator with X-Ray Volume Imaging (XVI) system
R3.5 or later, and the treatment table has been re-calibrated, then the XVI table factors must be
put into the XVI computer. See the applicable XVI Corrective Maintenance Manual.
When you do work on the digital accelerator, it can be necessary to calibrate the movement of
the gantry or the treatment table. The software for the digital accelerator does not automatically
send this changed calibration data to the XVI software. This causes errors to occur when XVI
calculates the table correction values, because the calibration data does not agree.
Therefore, it is important that you use the XVI DCB utility in the XVI software to copy the
applicable changed calibration data to the XVI software.
WARNING 3.1
Do not change the calibration of the gantry or treatment table movement, unless you copy the
applicable changed calibration data to the XVI software. Use the XVI DCB utility in the XVI
software to copy the changed calibration data. If you ignore this warning, you can cause clinical
mistreatment.
For the treatment table and gantry calibration changes in the XVI software, (see Table 3.15).

4.1 Common procedures for the treatment table
4.1.1 Disabling the movement system interlock
1 Log on to Service mode in Integrity™.
2 Click the Service Functions button.
3 Click the Override Machine Item button.
4 Set the Interlock Group to 3 - No Term. Movm.
Related Links:
Enabling the movement system interlock on page 119
4.1.2 Enabling the movement system interlock
1 Log on to Service mode in Integrity™.
2 Click the Override Machine Item button.
3 Set the Interlock Group to 1.
Related Links:
Disabling the movement system interlock on page 119
4.1.3 Installing the bellows cover of the treatment table
1 Remove the user interface modules.
2 Install the lower bellows cover (3). (See Figure 4.1). Make sure that the cutout in the lower
bellows cover faces the user interface module end of the treatment table.

4.1.4 Lowering the bellows cover of the treatment table
To get access to the movement assemblies of the treatment table to do the troubleshooting or
maintenance, it is necessary to lower the bellows cover.
There are two possible positions for the treatment table at the start of this procedure. The
treatment table can be as near as is possible to its lowest position, or it can be at a higher
position if motorized movement is not possible. At the lowest position, it is possible for one
person to lower the bellows cover. At a higher position, two persons must lower the bellows
cover.
1 Rotate the gantry to 0°.
2 If necessary, retract the MV and kV imaging arms.
3 If motorized movement is possible, move the treatment table as near as is possible to its
lowest position (see Figure 4.5). If motorized movement is not possible, go to step 4 .

4.1.7 Removing the height safety support strut from the treatment table
CAUTION 4.1
Do not move the treatment table when the safety support strut is installed. If you ignore this
caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
1 Connect the height drive motor (PL825C) to the power supply.
2 Remove the four high-tensile bolts that holds the safety support strut to the height
mechanism. If necessary, attach the manual crank handle to adjust the position of the height
mechanism and to make it easier to remove the screws. Do not discard the bolts.
3 Remove the safety support strut from the treatment table.
4 Install the bellows cover.

Common procedures for the treatment table
4.1.8 Using the manual crank handle
If there are no motorized movements, operate the manual crank handle to lift or lower the
treatment table.
1 Lower the bellows cover.
2 Attach the manual crank handle to the output shaft of the height motor.
001898 ©2013 Elekta Limited
Figure 4.17 Manual crank handle on the height motor shaft.
3 Release the height motor brake. Follow one of these two procedures.
a Use the brake release tool.
b Attach a length of string or wire to the brake release handle.
4 Pull the string or wire away from the brake release handle and along the axis of the height
motor.
5 Turn the manual crank handle clockwise to lift the treatment table, or counterclockwise to
lower the treatment table.
Note: 800 turns of the crank handle is approximately equal to 1000 mm of movement.
6 When the treatment table is in position, disengage the manual crank handle from the shaft
of the height motor.
Note: 800 turns of the crank handle is approximately equal to 1000 mm of movement.
WARNING 4.4
Do not use motorized movement of the treatment table until after you remove the manual
crank handle. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
7 If you used a string or wire to release the brake, remove these from the brake release
handle.

4.9.1 Correcting an LED 1 fault indication on LED display 1 to 4
This is the recommended procedure to use when LED 1 on LED display 1 to 4 shows that there is
a PWRUPFLT fault condition. LED 1 on LED display 1 to 4 will be ON if the LCU PCB firmware
senses a demand signal from the digital accelerator during power up (see step 1 ) or if there is no
enable signal from the host machine during table movement (see step 2 ). LED 1 on LED display 1
to 4 will be OFF if there is no PWRUPFLT fault condition.
1 Use the table that follows to do the troubleshooting if the LCU PCB firmware senses a
demand signal from the host machine during power up.
If Then
LED 1 is ON at power up
1 Examine these item part values for an output:
– Item 549 for longitudinal movement
– Item 540 for lateral movement
– Item 551 for height movement
– Item 548 for iso-rotation movement.
LED 1 is ON at power up,
1 Make sure that the HHC 1 and HHC 2 function demands
and there is an output for
operate correctly.
the item part values

4.9.2 Correcting an LED 2 fault indication on LED display 1 to 4
This procedure is used when LED 2 on LED display 1 to 4 shows that there is an ENBLCU fault
condition. LED 2 on LED display 1 to 4 will be ON if the LCU PCB sends enable signals to the
treatment table when movement starts. LED 2 on LED display 1 to 4 will be OFF if there is an
ENBLCU fault condition.
1 Use the table that follows to do the troubleshooting if there is an ENBLCU fault condition.
If Then
LED 2 is ON No correction is necessary.
LED 2 is OFF
1 Examine the operation of the LCU PCB.
Note: LED 2 only comes on if there is an enable signal from the LCU PCB.

4.9.3 Correcting an LED 3 fault indication on LED display 1 to 4
This is the recommended procedure to use when LED 3 on LED display 1 to 4 shows that there is
an ENBFLT fault condition. LED 3 on LED display 1 to 4 will be ON if there is a fault in the enable
circuits, which can be the result of a fault on the LTU. LED 3 on LED display 1 to 4 will be OFF if
there is no ENBFLT fault condition.

– For iso-rotation, 1B:B3 ENBYLTU

4.9.4 Correcting an LED 4 fault indication on LED display 1 to 4
This is the recommended procedure to use when LED 4 on LED display 1 to 4 shows that there is
an DEMFLT fault condition. LED 4 on LED display 1 to 4 will be ON if the voltage is incorrect when
the thumbwheel is in its center position. LED 4 on LED display 1 to 4 will be OFF if there is no
DEMFLT fault condition.
1 Use the table that follows to do the troubleshooting if there is an DEMFLT fault condition.
If Then
LED 4 is ON
1 Make sure that there are +10 V and 0 V power supplies
to the thumbwheels.
2 Make sure that the voltage with the thumbwheel in its
center position is +5 V ±10 mV.
3 If the voltage with the thumbwheel in its center position
is not +5 V ±10 mV, adjust the voltage.
LED 4 remains ON

– For longitudinal, 2B:B19 VELXPSSLTU
– For lateral, 2B:B21 VELXPSSLTU
– For iso-rotation, 2B:B25 VELXPSSLTU
– For height, 2B:B23 VELXPSSLTU.

4.9.5 Correcting an LED 5 fault indication on LED display 1 to 4
This is the recommended procedure to use when LED 5 on LED display 3 shows that there is a
MOVFLT fault condition for the height movement. LED 5 on LED display 3 will be ON if the LCU
senses a height movement when there is no valid demand signal. LED 5 on LED display 3 will be
OFF if there is no MOVFLT fault condition.
1 Use the table that follows to do the troubleshooting if there is an MOVFLT fault condition for
the height movement.
If Then
LED 5 is ON
1 Examine the operation of the brake.
The brake operates
1 Examine the voltage of the check and course potentiometer.
correctly
– If the voltage is stable, the LTU PCB can be the cause of the
MOVFLT fault condition. If necessary, replace the LTU PCB.
– If the voltage is not stable, replace the defective
potentiometer.
The brake does not
1 Set the brake gap to 0.2 mm.
operate correctly
2 If necessary, replace the brake assembly.
LED 5 is OFF No correction is necessary.

Troubleshooting
Troubleshooting for LED fault indications on LED display 1 to 4
4.9.6 Correcting an LED 6 fault indication on LED display 1 to 4
This is the recommended procedure to use when LED 6 on LED display 1 to 4 shows that there is
a CHKFLT fault condition. LED 6 on LED display 1 to 4 will be ON if there is a difference in the
voltage of more than 200 mV between the course potentiometer and the check potentiometer.
LED 6 on LED display 1 to 4 will be OFF if there is no CHKFLT fault condition.
This fault condition can occur in all movement systems. The possible causes for this fault
condition are:
• Wiring faults
• Incorrect voltages across the potentiometer drive
• Defective potentiometers
• Defective cables
• Drive belts move from their usual position and become non-linear.
1 Use the table that follows to do the troubleshooting if there is a CHKFLT fault condition.
If Then
LED 6 is ON
1 Make sure that there is power to the coarse potentiometer and the
check potentiometer.
2 Make sure that the drive belts are in the correct position.
3 Make sure that the movement of the potentiometers is linear through
its full range of movement.
LED 6 is OFF No correction is necessary.
Related Links:
LED bar graph displays on page 432
4.9.7 Correcting an LED 7 fault indication on LED display 1 to 4
This is the recommended procedure to use when LED 7 on LED display 1 to 4 shows that there is
a HIFLT fault condition. LED 7 on LED display 1 to 4 will be ON if the treatment table movement is
at a high voltage firmware limit (which is usual when the movement starts). LED 7 on LED display
1 to 4 will be OFF if there is no HIFLT fault condition.
1 Use the table that follows to do the troubleshooting if there is a HIFLT fault condition.
If Then
LED 7 is ON
1 Move the treatment table back out of the limit.
LED 7 is OFF No correction is necessary.
Note: The treatment table cannot move further than the limit.
Related Links:
LED bar graph displays on page 432

Service parts repair and replacement
Repair and replacement of the floor pan
5.1 Repair and replacement of the floor pan
5.1.1 Removing the floor pan with motorized movement of the treatment
table
Prerequisites
Hoist
Lifting jig
Lifting straps.
If motorized movement of the treatment table is available, you can use the treatment table to
help you to remove the floor pan. If motorized movement of the treatment table is not available,
do the procedure to remove the floor pan without motorized movement.
Before you try this procedure, make sure that you can use this procedure with third-party table
tops.
1 Rotate the column assembly to 0°.
2 Move the lateral carriage assembly to 0 mm.
3 Move treatment table in the longitudinal direction until the table top is above the rotating
floor pan.
4 Attach the straps to the table top and hang the lifting jig on the straps. Make sure that the
position of the bolt on the lifting jig is more in the direction of the treatment table.
5 On the user interface module, operate the HEIGHT thumbwheel until the lifting jig is above
the center of the floor pan.
6 Attach the lifting jig to the floor pan with the bolt. If necessary, operate the LATERAL and
LONGITUDINAL thumbwheels to carefully align the lifting jig to the floor pan.
7 On the user interface module, operate the ISO-ROTATION BRAKE button to release the iso-
rotation brake.
8 If the ISO-ROTATION BRAKE button does not release the iso-rotation brake:
a Remove the floorboard to get access to the brake.
b Tighten the nut (see Figure 5.1) on the iso-rotation brake to release the brake.

Do not let the floor pan tilt when you lift it. If the floor pan tilts, it can catch on the brake,
which will prevent free movement. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the
equipment.
9 On the user interface module, operate the HEIGHT thumbwheel to lift the treatment table
and the floor pan (see Figure 5.2).

5.1.2 Removing the floor pan without motorized movement of the
treatment table
Prerequisites
Hoist
Lifting jig
Lifting straps.
If motorized movement of the treatment table is not available, do this procedure to remove the
floor pan. If motorized movement of the treatment table is available, remove the floor pan with
motorized movement.
1 Lower the bellows cover.
2 On the user interface modulate, operate the ISO-ROTATION BRAKE button to release the iso-
rotation brake.
3 Use the manual crank handle to lift and lower the treatment table.
4 Install the lifting jig on the floor pan. Make sure that the floor pan jig aligns correctly with
the floor pan.

Service parts repair and replacement
Repair and replacement of the floor pan
WARNING 5.1
Do not lift or move heavy parts unless you obey the applicable Health and Safety regulations
and procedures. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
CAUTION 5.2
Do not let the floor pan tilt when you lift it. If the floor pan tilts, it can catch on the brake,
which will prevent free movement. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the
equipment.
5 Attach the lifting straps to the hoist and the lifting jig.
6 Carefully lift the floor pan clear of the iso-rotation assembly, and put it safely away from the
work area.
7 Remove the lifting straps from the floor pan and lifting jig.
Related Links:
Lowering the bellows cover of the treatment table on page 122
Using the manual crank handle on page 130
5.1.3 Installing the floor pan
Prerequisites
Hoist
Lifting jig
Lifting straps.
There is a locating pin on the iso-rotation assembly that operates the floor pan. This locating pin
must engage with the slot in the center of the floor pan during installation.
1 Lift the floor pan and make sure that it is above the iso-rotation assembly. Use motorized
movement or manual movement.
CAUTION 5.3
Do not install the floor pan with the iso-rotation brake engaged. Release the iso-rotation brake
before you install the floor pan. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the brake
pads.
2 Press the ISO-ROTATION BRAKE button to release the iso-rotation brake.
3 If necessary, remove the bolt (1) from the transit locking hole in the drive platter.

4 Lower the floor pan on to the iso-rotation assembly.
a Make sure that the locating pin on the iso-rotation assembly is fully engaged with the
slot in the floor pan.
b Make sure that the floor pan is correctly installed in to the iso-rotation brake unit. The
rim of the floor pan must fit between the two brake pads.
c Make sure that the rim of the floor pan is supported equally by the six floor pan
support rollers.
5 If necessary, install removed parts.
a Install the floorboards.
b Install the bellows cover.
6 When all other work on the treatment table is completed, and the treatment table is
assembled for usual operation, examine the operation of the iso-rotation assembly.

treatment table to get easy access to the top covers.
3 Disconnect the electrical supply to the treatment table.

5.3.2 Installing the iso-rotation drive subassembly
When you install a new iso-rotation drive subassembly, it is important to set the potentiometers
correctly. This procedure makes sure that the wipers of the coarse and check potentiometers are
set to less than 20 mV of each other, to give correct system operation.
1 Put the bolt (1) in the transit locking hole to lock the iso-rotation drive subassembly at 0°
(see Figure 5.30)
1
001844_001
Figure 5.30 Location of the transit locking hole on the iso-rotation drive subassembly
Precise Treatment Table™ Document ID: 45133701862 10
Corrective Maintenance Manual
©2019 Elekta Limited. Page 189

Service parts repair and replacement
Repair and replacement of the iso-rotation drive subassembly
(1) M10 bolt in the transit locking hole
2 Put the new iso-rotation drive assembly in position on the triangular base.
3 Connect the potentiometer connectors to PL2, PL3, and PL4 of PCB812.
4 Switch on the EIM cabinet.
5 Measure and record the supply voltage across the wiper of the coarse potentiometer on pin
1 and pin 3 of PL2 on PCB812.
6 Measure and record the voltage across the wiper of the coarse potentiometer on pin 2 and
pin 1 of PL2 on PCB812.
7 Rotate the drive gear of the coarse potentiometer to adjust the measured voltage until it is
equal to the half of the supply voltage (±10 mV).
8 Measure the voltage across the wiper of the check potentiometer on pin 2 and pin 1 of PL4
on PCB812.
9 Adjust the check potentiometer as follows until the measured voltage is the same as the
coarse potentiometer (±20 mV):
a Rotate the drive gear.
b Rotate the body of the potentiometer (for small adjustments).
c Move the belt on the potentiometer by one tooth (40 mV change in measured voltage).
10 Put the iso-rotation drive belt through the drive gears (see Figure 5.31).
000011885577 ©©22001100 EElleekkttaa LLiimmiitteedd
Figure 5.31 Iso-rotation drive gears and drive belt
11 Make sure that the voltages that you set in step 7 and step 9 have not changed, the two
voltages must be the same (±20 mV)
12 Hold the drive belt against the gears, and push the drive subassembly to its correct position.
13 Install the four screws at the corners of the drive subassembly.
14 Connect PL2 and PL4 (for the motor and clutch) to PCB811.
15 Attach the cables to the triangular base with cable clamps.
16 Remove the bolt (1) from the transit locking hole (see Figure 5.30).
17 When all other work on the treatment table is completed, and the treatment table is
assembled for usual operation, examine the operation of the iso-rotation drive assembly.
18 Calibrate the iso-rotation system.

5.3.4 Installing the timing belt of the iso-rotation drive subassembly
1 Put the timing belt on the drive gears of the iso-rotation drive subassembly.
2 Move the drive motor and clutch on the lower plate until the tension on the timing belt
gives 4 mm (±1 mm) free play between the two drive gears (see Figure 5.34).

Figure 5.34 Tension on the timing belt of the iso-rotation drive subassembly
3 Rotate the drive shaft of the motor by hand and make sure that the output shaft of the
clutch does not also rotate.
If the clutch shaft rotates, the timing belt is too tight and the force on the drive shaft makes
the clutch drag.

5.3.7 Installing the iso-rotation drive motor and clutch
You can find the iso-rotation drive motor and clutch in the iso-rotation drive subassembly.
1 Install the motor and clutch on the base plate of the iso-rotation drive subassembly.
2 Install the lower drive gear on the motor drive shaft.
a Make sure that you put the two set screws on the flat surface of the drive shaft.
b Make sure that the distance between the gear and the mounting plate is 0.5 mm.
3 Apply Loctite® 222™ and tighten the two set screws.
4 Install the timing belt of the iso-rotation motor drive.
5 Install a new cable tie to hold the cable of the drive motor and clutch to a pillar in the drive
subassembly.
6 Install the iso-rotation drive subassembly on the triangular base.
7 Install the primary drive belt on the iso-rotation drive subassembly.
8 Examine the operation of the iso-rotation drive assembly.

4 Tighten the Nyloc nut (3) at the bottom of the brake unit to open the jaws of the brake.
5 Install the rotating floor pan.
6 Adjust the height of the brake unit until the brake pads touch the rim of the floor pan.
7 Tighten, but do not fully tighten the four screws that hold the brake unit to the iso-rotation
assembly.
8 Loosen the Nyloc nut (3) fully to close the jaws of the brake.
9 Adjust the top brake pad adjustment screw (1), until the brake material touches the rim of

brake unit find the position in which the rim is in the center of the brake unit.

the rim of the floor pan.
12 Fully tighten the four screws that hold the brake unit to the iso-rotation assembly.
13 Press the ISO-ROTATION BRAKE button on one of the user interface modules to energize the
brake.
14 Adjust the top brake pad adjustment screw (1) to give the minimum clearance with the floor
pan, but continues to let the iso-rotation drive operate through its full range of travel.
15 Press the ISO-ROTATION BRAKE button to de-energize the brake.
16 Use a spring balance and make sure that the brake force is more than 220 Nm (22.4 kg
measured at a distance of one meter (1m) from the pivot point).
17 Adjust the brake pads or the position of the brake unit until there is motorized movement
and a brake force of 220 Nm, through the full range of iso-rotation.
18 Install the G-B floorboard. Make sure that the brake unit does not touch the bottom of the
floorboard.
19 Examine the operation of the iso-rotation drive assembly.

5.6 Repair and replacement of the iso-rotation interconnection
PCB
5.6.1 Removing the iso-rotation interconnection PCB
There are two interconnection PCBs, one on the A-side (PCB811) and the other on the B-side
(PCB812) of the iso-rotation triangular base. The same procedure applies to each PCB.
1 Remove the floor pan.
CAUTION 5.5
Do not touch the printed circuit boards (PCBs) and other electronic parts unless you wear a
grounded antistatic wrist strap. If you do not wear a wrist strap you can get an electrostatic
discharge, and this can also have an effect of the operation of the PCB. If you ignore this
caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
2 Switch off the mains power supply at the EIM.
3 Put on an antistatic wrist strap and connect it to a ground (earth) point.
4 Disconnect connectors PL1 and SK1 from the PCB (see Figure 5.49).

5.6.2 Installing the iso-rotation interconnection PCB
There are two interconnection PCBs, one on the A-side (PCB811) and the other on the B-side
(PCB812) of the iso-rotation triangular base. The same procedure applies to both PCBs.
CAUTION 5.6
Do not touch the PCBs and other electronic parts unless you wear a grounded antistatic wrist
strap. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
1 Put the PCB in its position on the triangular base.
2 Install the four screws, plain washers, spring washers, and spacers that hold the PCB to the
triangular base.
3 Connect connectors PL2, PL3, and PL4 to the PCB.
4 Connect connectors PL1 and SK1 to the PCB. Make sure that the catch on the connector
engages the connection on the PCB.
5 Install the floor pan.
6 Switch on the mains electrical supply CB1 at the EIM cabinet.
7 When all other work on the treatment table is completed, and the treatment table is
assembled for usual operation, examine the operation of the iso-rotation drive assembly.

Draw wire

5.7.3 Installing the column rotation brake magnet coil
Prerequisites
Draw wire
1 Disassemble plug PL815F on the new brake magnet coil.
2 To prevent damage to the pins of the cable, put tape around the pins before you route it
through the column rotation casting.
3 Attach the draw wire to the end of the cable of the brake magnet coil.
4 Carefully pull the draw wire to pull the cable of the new brake magnet coil though the
column rotation casting.
5 Assemble plug PL815F on the cable of the brake magnet coil.
6 Make sure that the pins are in the correct positions and they terminate correctly. The brown
cable must connect to pin 1 and the blue cable must connect to pin 2.
7 Install the column rotation brake unit.

Installing the column rotation brake unit on page 209

5.7.4 Installing the column rotation brake unit
1 Assemble the spring washers and the spring pin into the recess in the column foot casting
for the brake magnet coil. To help with the assembly, align the springs away from the
column bearing.
Note: The spring washers must be replaced in the same sequence that was recorded during removal
procedure.
2 Lower the brake unit on the springs and install the four screws that hold the column rotation
brake unit. Do not tighten the screws. If you tighten the screws, adjustment of the brake will
not be possible.
3 Put the eccentric adjuster in the center of the hole and make sure that it engages with the
casting and that you can rotate it.
4 Adjust the column rotation brake unit.

Service parts repair and replacement
Repair and replacement of the column rotation brake unit
The column rotation brake unit is at the bottom of the column casting. You can get access to the
column rotation brake unit through the cover plate at the bottom of the column.
1 Make sure that the four screws that hold the column rotation brake unit are loose.
2 On the user interface modulate, operate the COLUMN BRAKE button to give an electrical
supply to the coil of the brake magnet. Make sure that the indicator lamp in the COLUMN
BRAKE button goes off.
3 Adjust the spring pressure screw (1) until the brake activates (see Figure 5.55. If necessary,
loosen the spring pressure screw to adjust it. Make sure that the brake lever bonds
magnetically to the magnet coil.
4 Turn the eccentric adjuster (2) until the friction plate material of the brake touches the
column bearing.
5 Fully tighten the four screws to hold the brake unit at the adjusted position.
6 Adjust the set screw (3) to get small adjustments in the air gap between the rubber of the
brake and the column bearing. Tighten the lock nut (4) after the adjustment.

5.10.3 Adjusting the microswitch PCB on the column indent mechanism
If the treatment table is not at 0°, the buzzer PCB activates and makes a sound.
Note: You must make sure that the microswitch PCB is set up correctly and in specification before
clinical operation.
The audible indication stops approximately ±5° from the 0° indent position. For example, when
you rotate the column to 0°, you hear a sound when you are approximately ±5° away from 0°.
This stops when in the 0° indent position. Equally, if you rotate the column away from 0°, you
hear a sound when the column is at approximately ±5°. After ±5°, the sound stops.
Note: It is important that the treatment table is set up correctly on the inner edge (2) to give an audible
indication at 0.13° (see Figure 5.62).
1 Attach the column indent alignment template to the G-end of the treatment table.
2 Make sure that the projection of the crosswire image aligns with the crosswires on the
template.
3 Move the column rotation between ±5° from the indent position (1) (see Figure 5.62).

5.11.2 Installing the service end stops on the longitudinal carriage
assembly
The installation of the service end stops lets the longitudinal carriage assembly move more than
its usual range of movement in the G-direction. This gives full access to the longitudinal drive
mechanism and brake units, and it prevents the separation of the longitudinal carriage assembly
from the treatment table.
CAUTION 5.8
Do not move the lateral or longitudinal carriage assembly more than its usual range of
movement because some of the roller bearings can disengage from the bearing channels. If you
ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
1 At the user interface module, press the LAT/LONG BRAKE (or LONGITUDINAL BRAKE) button
to apply the longitudinal brake.

assembly (see Figure 5.71).

5.11.3 Installing the longitudinal carriage assembly

between the rails.

brake-band screws.

brake-bands to the longitudinal carriage assembly (see Figure 5.77). Make sure that you
install the shims in the positions identified in the removal procedure.

5.17.2 Installing the service end stops on the lateral carriage assembly
The installation of the service end stops lets the lateral carriage assembly move more than its
usual range of movement in the B-direction. This gives full access to the lateral drive mechanism
and brake units on the A-side of the lateral carriage assembly. This prevents the separation of the
lateral carriage assembly from the treatment table.
CAUTION 5.9
Do not move the lateral or longitudinal carriage assembly more than its usual range of
movement because some of the roller bearings can disengage from the bearing channels. If you
ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
1 On the user interface module, press the LAT/LONG BRAKE (or LATERAL BRAKE) button to
release the longitudinal brake.

5.18.3 Adjusting the clutch of the lateral drive subassembly
Prerequisites
Feeler gauge.
Loctite® 243™
1 Remove the lateral cover set.
2 Install the service end stops.
3 Remove the lateral drive subassembly.
4 Loosen the two set screws that attach the hub of the clutch pressure plate to the output
shaft of the gearbox.
5 Use a feeler gauge to set an air gap of 0.2 mm between the surface of the clutch and the
surface of the pressure plate.
6 Tighten the set screws, and then use the feeler gauge to make sure that the air gap is
0.2 mm.
7 If the air gap is not 0.2 mm, do step 4 to step 6 again until the air gap is 0.2 mm.
8 Replace the set screws.
a Remove a set screw from the hub.
b Remove and clean old Loctite® adhesive from the screw threads.
c Apply Loctite® 243™ to the screw threads of the set screw.
d Install the set screw in the hub.
e Do step 8 a to d for the other set screw.
9 Install the lateral drive subassembly.
10 Remove the service end stops.
11 Install the lateral cover set.

5.20.1 Removing a potentiometer from the lateral movement system
1 Lower the bellows cover.
2 Remove the lateral cover set.
3 Install the service end stops.
4 Move the lateral drive plate to the centre position, and align the holes in the lateral drive
plate with the holes in the lateral drive subassembly.
5 Install two screws to lock the lateral drive plate in position.
6 At the TCC switch off the mains power supply to the EIM cabinet.
7 At PCB835, below the height mechanism interface plate, disconnect the applicable plugs:
– PL3 (lateral movement coarse potentiometer)
– PL4 (lateral movement fine potentiometer)
– PL5 (lateral movement check potentiometer).
8 Loosen the set screw on the shaft of the potentiometer.
9 Remove the nut and washers, and remove the unserviceable potentiometer. Record the
installed position of the washers.

5.20.2 Installing a potentiometer in the lateral movement system
Prerequisites
Digital voltmeter.
1 Move the lateral drive plate to the center position, and align the holes in the lateral drive
plate with the holes in the lateral drive subassembly.
2 Install two screws to lock the lateral drive plate in position.
3 At PCB835, connect the cable to the applicable plug:
– PL3 (lateral movement coarse potentiometer)
– PL4 (lateral movement fine potentiometer)
– PL5 (lateral movement check potentiometer).
4 Adjust the new potentiometer until the voltage is equal to 5 V (±10 mV).
5 Put the potentiometer in position and install the nut and washer to attach the
potentiometer to the bracket.
Note: Do not fully tighten the nut.
6 Apply a small quantity of Loctite® 243™ to the threads of the set screw, and tighten the set
screw on the shaft of the potentiometer.
7 Measure the voltage of the coarse or check potentiometer between pin 1 and 2.
8 Rotate the drive gear of the new potentiometer to adjust the measured voltage until it is
equal to 5 V (±10 mV).
Note: For a new fine potentiometer, you do not have to adjust the voltage.
9 Tighten the 13mm nut and make sure that the voltage is equal to 5 V (±10 mV).
10 Remove the two screws that lock the lateral drive plate in position.
11 Install the longitudinal drive subassembly.
12 Remove the safety support struct.
13 Assemble the longitudinal carriage assembly.
14 Do the calibration of the longitudinal movement system.

5.26.1.2 Installing the height motor drive on the height drive gearbox
Prerequisites
Height drive motor toolkit.
This procedure is only applicable to treatment tables before Serial No. 126499, with an
Electrocraft motor.
1 Remove unwanted grease from the coupling on the gearbox.
2 Remove the flexible element from the coupling of the gearbox or the motor. Keep the
flexible element.
3 Loosen the set screw that holds the coupling on the old height drive motor shaft.
4 Remove the old coupling and flexible element from the motor shaft.
Note: To remove the old coupling from the motor shaft can be difficult. If necessary, apply force to
remove it.
5 Lubricate the new motor shaft with a thin layer of EP2 grease.
6 Install the coupling on the new motor shaft.
7 Move the coupling along the motor shaft until you can see the end of the motor shaft
through the coupling. Do not tighten the set screw at this time.
8 Align the coupling on the motor shaft with the coupling in the gearbox shaft. Make sure that
the jaws of each coupling do not cause a collision.
9 Put the motor into the gearbox and make sure that the front face of the motor touches with
the front face of the gearbox.
This will set the coupling on the motor shaft to the correct start position.
10 Remove the motor from the gearbox shaft, and then move the coupling on the motor shaft
1 mm in the direction of the motor.

5.28.2 Disconnecting the electrical parts from the height drive gearbox
1 Attach the manual crank handle to the shaft of the height drive motor.
2 Carefully turn the manual crank handle a maximum of one to two turns in the counter-
clockwise direction to release the tension on the ballscrews.
The height drive motor will be free to move.
3 In relation to 0 V at PL2 on PCB825A, measure the wiper voltage of the coarse
potentiometer (orange connector). Record the measured value.
4 In relation to 0 V at PL4 on PCB825A, measure the wiper voltage of the check potentiometer
(orange connector). Record the measured value.
5 At the EIM cabinet, switch off the power at CB1.
6 Disconnect the height motor connector PL825C.
7 Disconnect PL2, PL3 and PL4 from PCB825A.
8 Disconnect the height movement brake connector (SK3) from PCB825B.
9 Make a note of the positions of the potentiometer cables.
10 Remove the cable clips and the cable ties that attach the cables to the height mechanism.
11 Remove the two screws that attach the bracket of the upper limit microswitch to the upper
surface of the gearbox.
12 Make sure that the microswitch and its wires are clear of the height drive mechanism.
13 To get access to the cup support pin on the upper ballscrew, temporarily disconnect and
move SKT830B and PL830E clear of the height mechanism. The cables are on the A-side of
the height mechanism.

5.28.4 Releasing the height drive gearbox from the height mechanism
Prerequisites
A piece of wood (50 mm high × 100 mm wide × 1000 mm long).
CAUTION 5.11
Do not let the ball screw cups turn when you retract the ball screws. If the ball screw cups turn,
they can catch on the height mechanism and prevent a smooth retraction. If you ignore this
caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
1 Make sure that the ball screw cup does not rotate. Use the manual crank handle and turn
the gearbox input shaft of the gearbox in the counterlockwise direction.
The ball screws retract to give sufficient clearance to remove the height drive gearbox.
WARNING 5.7
Do not loosen the screws until you put a piece of wood in the correct position to hold the
weight of the height drive gearbox. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
2 Continue to turn the gearbox input shaft until you have sufficient clearance to put a piece of
wood below the lower ball screw.
Note: You will feel an increased resistance through the manual crank handle when the ball screws
retract. To prevent blockage, align each ball screw cup at the center at each turn of the crank
handle.
3 Put the piece of wood below the lower ball screw to hold the height drive gearbox.
NOT TO SCALE 001919 ©2010 Elekta Limited
Figure 5.179 Wood below the lower ball screw to support the height movement gearbox
4 Attach a cable tie to the screw thread of each ball screw, immediately above and below the
height drive gearbox.
This will prevent the ball screws from becoming loose on their own weight when you
remove the height drive gearbox from the height drive mechanism.

5.28.5 Removing the roller bearing from the height drive gearbox
Prerequisites
22 mm socket, with a wrench and short extension bar
1 Move the height drive gearbox to a position that gives you access to the screw on the A-side
of the gearbox (see Figure 5.180).
2 Use a 22 mm socket with a wrench and a short extension bar to remove the M12 bolt as
follows.
Note: The Loctite® adhesive on the screws can also make them difficult to loosen. It is possible that
some leverage will be necessary to pull the screw out of the bearing guide.
a Make sure that the splines of the socket engage the head of the screw correctly.
b If the socket does not engage with the head of the screw correctly, remove some of the
end of the socket until it does.
c Remove the screw.
3 Remove the roller bearing and keep the screw and the spacer washer.

5.28.7 Removing the height drive gearbox
Be careful when you remove the height drive gearbox. The height drive gearbox is heavy. It
weighs approximately 15 kg and can be difficult to move. More than one person is necessary to
lift the height drive gearbox.
WARNING 5.9
Do not lift or move heavy parts unless you obey the applicable Health and Safety regulations
and procedures. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
1 If necessary, loosen the center bolt of the safety support strut to give the necessary
clearance to remove the gearbox.
2 Remove the height drive gearbox from the three-angle box. Make sure that the:
– Ball screws do not rotate
– Linear bearings do not come off their guide track.
3 Remove the breather plug from the defective height drive gearbox.
4 Remove the blanking plug from the replacement height drive gearbox.
