5.28.9 Preparing the height drive gearbox for installation
1 At the EIM cabinet, switch off the mains power supply at CB1.
2 Install the potentiometers, gears, and brackets on the gearbox. See the figures that follow:
– For the installation of the check potentiometer RV825A on the top of the gearbox (see
Figure 5.185).
– For the installation of the fine and coarse potentiometers RV825B and RV825C on the
bottom of the gearbox (see Figure 5.186).
a Install the wiring in the routing shown in Figure 5.185 and Figure 5.186.
b Engage the potentiometer gears with the output gears of the gearbox, to minimize
backlash on the potentiometer.

5.28.11 Attaching the ball screw cups to the upper and lower arms
Prerequisites
Manual crank handle
1 Release the height movement brake.
2 Use the manual crank handle to lift or lower the ball screw cups, until the pivot bearings
align with the holes in the side of the height mechanism.
3 Attach one of the pivot pins to the threaded rod.
4 Push the pivot pin through the side of the arm and into the pivot bearing in the ball screw
cup. Make sure that the pivot pin engages to the correct depth with the pivot bearing, and
that it aligns correctly with the tapped hole.
Note: You can use the manual crank handle to lift the gearbox to engage the pivot pins.

5.28.12 Connecting the electrical parts to the height drive gearbox
1 Install PCB830A on the A-side of the height mechanism. Make sure that the wires stay
connected.
2 Attach the bracket of the upper limit microswitch and its wires to the upper surface of the
gearbox.
3 Connect the potentiometers to PL 2, PL 3, and PL 4 of PCB825A.
4 At the EIM cabinet, switch on the mains power supply at CB1.
5 Make sure that the voltages of the coarse potentiometers are:
– The same as the value recorded before the gearbox was removed
– The difference between the coarse and check potentiometers is less than 20mV.
Note: You can turn the body of the potentiometer to set the end value of the output voltage accuracy.
6 Attach cable clips and cable ties where necessary to safely hold the cables and wires of the
height drive gearbox, motor, and potentiometers.
7 Connect the height motor connector PL825C.
8 Remove the safety support strut.
9 Connect the height movement brake connector (SK3) to PCB825B.
10 Make sure that the height mechanism operates correctly.
a Move the height mechanism through its full range of movement.
b Make sure that the height mechanism does not activate an upper or lower limit switch.

5.29 Repair and replacement of the height movement
potentionmeters - early configuration
5.29.1 Description of the height movement potentiometer configurations
There are two different configurations of readback potentiometers, earlier and later, for the
height movement system. The earlier configuration is applicable to treatment tables before Serial
No. 125137.
CAUTION 5.12
Do not let the potentiometer cable touch the height mechanism during the installation of a
height movement potentiometer. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the
potentiometer.
Note: Make sure that there is no tension on the cable when you move the gearbox forward.
Height readback potentiometers - early configuration
The coarse and check potentiometer assembly (4513 331 7838) is on the top surface of the
height gearbox (see Figure 5.192).

5.29.2 Removing the coarse or check potentiometer - early configuration
Prerequisites
• Digital voltmeter
• Thin ½ inch A/F wrench (specially ground).
The coarse and check potentiometer assembly (4513 331 7838) is on the top surface of the
height gearbox. You must first remove the potentiometer nearest the upper ball screw before
you can remove the potentiometer nearest the limit microswitch.
1 Lower the bellows cover.
2 Move the treatment table up until you can get access to the height potentiometers, and
then press the STOP MOTORS button.
Note: If electrical power is not available, use the manual crank handle to raise the treatment table.
001971 ©2010 Elekta Limited
Figure 5.196 Removal of the potentiometer
3 Identify the defective potentiometer (coarse or check) on the upper surface of the height
gearbox.
4 At connector PL1 on PCB825A at the rear of the three angle box, measure and record the
voltage on:
– Pin 5 (coarse potentiometer wiper) in relation to 0 V at PL1, pin 1
– Pin 8 (check potentiometer wiper) in relation to 0 V at PL1, pin 3
– Make sure that the difference between the two output voltages is less than 20 mV.
5 If the defective potentiometer is that nearest to:
– The limit microswitch, do the steps that follow for the two potentiometers
– The upper ball screw, do the steps that follow for this potentiometer only.

– Release the two set screws that attach the drive gear to the shaft of the potentiometer.
– Remove the drive gear from the shaft of the potentiometer.
9 Remove the ½ inch A/F wrench and washers that attach the potentiometer to the bracket.

CAUTION 5.13
Do not use or continue to examine the treatment table if the output voltage of the
potentiometers decreases. The output voltage of the potentiometers increase when you lower
the treatment table. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the potentiometers.
2 Measure the voltages on PL2 and PL4 of PCB825A. Make sure that the voltages increase as
the treatment table lowers.
3 If the measured voltage decreases:
1 Manually turn the shaft of the potentiometer clockwise 10 turns to set the treatment
table to its initial position.
2 Find the cause of the error.
3 Correct the error.
Note: If you do not correct the error, it will cause damage to the potentiometers and a failure of the
treatment table.
4 On the user interface module, operate the HEIGHT thumbwheel to move the treatment
table to its lowest position. Make sure that:
1 The potentiometer and its cables do not make contact with parts that move.
2 The cables do not have too much tension in them through the full range of movement.
5 If the potentiometer is installed correctly, the table top will stop at the upper firmware limit
(lowest height position). This is approximately 660 mm above the finished floor level if the
treatment table has a C-Arm table top.
6 Use the HEIGHT thumbwheel to move the treatment table to its highest position (lower
firmware limit). Make sure that:
1 The potentiometer and its cables do not make contact with parts that move.
2 The cables do not have too much tension in them through the full range of movement.
Note: The correct setting of the potentiometer wiper voltages is very important for correct operation of
the treatment table. If the treatment table touches a limit switch and stops before the firmware
limit, the potentiometer wiper voltage is incorrect. If this occurs, you must adjust the wiper
voltage.
7 If necessary, do steps 1 to 6 again until the treatment table does not touch a limit switch.
8 Set the treatment table to the isocentric height.
9 Install the bellows cover.
10 Examine the operation of the height movement system.

5.31 Adjusting the height movement limit switches
5.31.1 Adjusting the upper limit switches of the height movement
mechanism
There are four limit switches and four adjustable actuators (two of each for the lower limit, and
the upper limit) installed on the height mechanism. The table top must be installed when you set
up the upper and lower limit switches.
1 Set the column rotation to 0°.
2 Lower the bellows cover.
3 Attach a length of string or wire to the brake release handle of the height motor, this will let
you manually release the brake.
4 On the user interface module, operate the HEIGHT thumbwheel to move the treatment
table up, until you can get access to the upper limit microswitches.
Note: The upper limit microswitches are on the gun side of the upper arm assembly.
5 Short circuit one of the two microswitches to disable its operation.
6 Use the HEIGHT thumbwheel to lower the treatment table to the upper firmware limit
(minimum table height).
7 Accurately measure the vertical distance between the upper surface of the table top and the
finished floor level. Record this distance as measurement H1.
Note: The reference voltage for the minimum height H1 is defined in the Firmware of the LCU PCB in the
EIM cabinet. If the coarse and check potentiometers of the height mechanism are set incorrectly,
the treatment table can move to a very low position. If this occurs, the treatment table will
operate the limit switches.
8 Pull the string or wire out along the axis of the height motor to manually release the brake.
9 Turn the manual crank handle counterclockwise until the upper microswitch limit (minimum
table height) activates.
On the user interface module, the LEDs go off for three to five seconds.
Document ID: 45133701862 10 Precise Treatment Table™
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 340 ©2019 Elekta Limited.

Service parts repair and replacement
Adjusting the height movement limit switches
10 Accurately measure the vertical distance between the upper surface of the table top and the
finished floor level. Record this distance as measurement H2.
11 Compare the measurements H1 and H2. If the coarse and check potentiometers are set
correctly, H1 will be 6 mm greater than H2.
12 Manually release the brake.
13 Turn the manual crank handle counterclockwise until the treatment table is at the upper
limit of mechanical travel.
14 At this point, you will feel an increased resistance as you turn the manual crank handle.
15 Accurately measure the vertical distance between the upper surface of the table top and the
finished floor level. Record this distance as measurement H3.
16 If the limit switches are set correctly, measurement H2 will be 2 mm to 3 mm greater than
H3 .
17 Manually release the brake.
WARNING 5.12
Do not use motorized movement of the treatment table until after you remove the manual
crank handle. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
18 Turn the manual crank handle clockwise to move the treatment table to a height that it is
10 mm above the value recorded as H2.
19 Reset the STOP MOTORS inhibit, and move the table up as necessary.
20 Do steps 8 to 11 again, and use the activating screw on the gun side of the lower arm to
adjust the microswitch. Adjust the microswitch until the value of H2 is 2 mm to 3 mm
greater than H3.
21 Remove the short circuit from the microswitch. Then short circuit the other upper limit
microswitch to disable its operation.
22 Do steps 8 to 16 again for the other microswitch.
23 Make sure that the value of measurement H1 is a minimum of 6 mm greater than H2. If it is
not, adjust the coarse and check potentiometers as necessary.
Note: If you adjust the coarse potentiometer, you must calibrate the height movement system.
24 If necessary, calibrate the height movement system.

3 Short circuit the microswitch installed on top of the height gearbox to disable its operation.
4 Manually release the brake.
5 Turn the manual crank handle clockwise until the lower microswitch limit (maximum table

17 Do steps 4 to 15 again for the other microswitch.

5.33 Connecting and disconnecting the EIM mains power supply
5.33.1 Disconnecting the electrical interface module from the electrical
supply
There is no electrical power supply to the EIM cabinet when the digital accelerator is off. But if it
is on, there is an electrical power supply to the EIM cabinet.
WARNING 5.14
Do not do work on the EIM if it is connected to the electrical power supply. Always disconnect
the electrical supply before you start the work. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal
injury.
WARNING 5.15
Do not do work on the EIM cabinet with the covers removed, until you disconnect the electrical
supply. There are high voltages at the UPS, although the electrical supply to the EIM cabinet is
disconnected. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
All the steps of this procedure must be completed to remove the electrical supply to the EIM
cabinet.
1 On the front panel of the EIM PSU, switch CB1 to OFF to disconnect the electrical supply to
the PSU.
The UPS continues to supply power to the PSU, and the amber lamp below CB1 stays on.
2 On the front panel of the UPS, press the OFF button to disconnect the electrical supply to
the PSU.
On the front panel of the EIM PSU, the amber lamp below CB1 goes off.
3 On the interface cabinet, switch CB13 to OFF to disconnect the electrical supply to the UPS.

5.38.3 Examining the operation of the emergency down drive
1 Start the digital accelerator to supply the 24VACHOST signal to the EIM cabinet.
2 Set the digital accelerator to the Preparatory machine state.
3 Move the treatment table to its highest position.
4 Operate the EMERGENCY STOP button in the treatment room wall to simulate a failure to
the electrical power supply.
This removes the electrical power supply and the 24VACHOST signal from the EIM cabinet.
5 Make sure that the operation of the ENABLE bar on each user interface module causes the
treatment table to move down.
6 After six minutes:
a Make sure that the LEDs in the buttons of each user interface module stay on.
This shows that the UPS has not shut down and the UPS battery is in a good condition.
b Make sure that the ENABLE bar of each user interface module causes the treatment
table to move down.
7 Reset the EMERGENCY STOP button in the treatment room.
This switches on the electrical power supply and supplies the 24VACHOST signal to the EIM
cabient.
8 Set the digital accelerator to the Preparatory machine state.
9 At the TCS, do a system shutdown.
This removes the electrical power supply to the EIM cabinet, but continues to supply the
24VACHOST signal.
10 Make sure that the LEDs in the buttons of each user interface module go off.
– If the EDD Timer Module is installed (machines before Serial No. 152531), the UPS shuts
down after approximately 5 minutes (±1 minute). If necessary, you can adjust this time
at the EDD timer module.
– If the EDD Timer Module is not installed (machines from Serial No. 152531), the UPS
shuts down after approximately 1 minute (±30 seconds).

Installing the EDD timer module in the electrical interface module on page 363

5.39.1 Examining the lateral movement system of the treatment table
1 On the user interface module, operate the LATERAL thumbwheel and make sure that:
a The lateral speed (i120) continuously changes between 3 mm/s to 45 mm/s.
b The treatment table moves in the A and B directions on command, and the direction of
movement agrees with the symbol above the thumbwheel.
c The treatment table moves in the A and B directions for a minimum of ±250 mm from
the center position.
d Listen for unusual sounds as the mechanism operates.

5.40 Calibrating the treatment table without Remote Automatic
Table Movement
5.40.1 Description of the calibration procedure for the Precise Treatment
Table™ without Remote Automatic Table Movement
Calibration of the treatment table is different for systems with or without Remote Automatic
Table Movement (RATM). The calibration procedures in this chapter are applicable for Integrity™
TCS, without RATM.
These procedures show the movements check for the longitudinal, lateral, height, and iso-
rotation of the treatment table.
These procedures are applicable after an upgrade from a RAM pit type treatment table to a
Precise Treatment Table™.
For all calibration procedures and movement calibration procedures, the gantry and the beam
limiting device must be set to 0°, unless the procedure tell you differently. The crosswires must
also be accurate.
Note: Elekta recommends that you do these procedures in the sequence they are written.
The procedures show:
• Software items used during the procedure
• Examining the movements before calibration
• Calibration procedures
• The post-calibration checks.
Use the Calibrate buttons on the secondary function toolbar to get access to the calibration
functions, see Figure 5.223 and Figure 5.224.
Figure 5.223 Calibrate button
Figure 5.224 Calibrate Movement button
WARNING 5.25
Do not start the treatment table calibration from the control room unless you can see that no
person is in the area near the treatment table. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious
injury and damage to the equipment.
WARNING 5.26
Do not save the calibration data until you make sure that all the parameters are correct. When
you save the calibration data, a full digital accelerator calibration is saved. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause clinical mistreatment.

5.40.2 Calibrating the longitudinal movement (single potentiometer)
This procedure is applicable for Integrity™ TCS, single potentiometer and without RATM. Do not

the G-direction.
6 Type 9000 in Cal value.

you save the calibration data, a full digital accelerator calibration is saved. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause clinical mistreatment.
8 Select Save LINAC Calibration.

in the correct direction and that the correct results are displayed. (See Figure 5.225).
Note: When the treatment table moves in the T-direction, the values decrease, and when the treatment
table moves in the G-direction, the values increase.

5.40.6 Calibrating the iso-rotation system (single potentiometer)
This procedure is applicable for Integrity™ TCS, a single potentiometer and without RATM. Do not
use this procedure to calibrate a system with Integrity™ R3.x and later.
The mechanical floor scale supplied with the iso-rotation system is set to the IEC 61217 standard.
But, you must use the bipolar scale convention to calibrate the iso-rotation system. The clinical
software will convert the machine scale conventions.
The floor scale of the treatment table has an IEC 60601 or IEC 61217 scale convention. But, the
calibration procedure uses bipolar coordinates. Bipolar coordinates are positive or negative
angles measured from 0°, for example, 90° or –90°.
In the instructions that follow, the positions of the IEC 61217 scale convention are in brackets.
1 Select the Calibration service page.
2 Type 110 in Cal.item 1.
3 Press the ISO-ROTATION thumbwheel to rotate the treatment table in the clockwise
direction to the +90° (270°) position.
4 Type 900 in Cal value.
5 Press the ISO-ROTATION thumbwheel to rotate the treatment table in the counterclockwise
direction to the -90° (+90°) position.
6 Type -900 in Cal value.
7 Type 0 in Cal.item 1.

warning, you can cause clinical mistreatment.
8 Select Save LINAC Calibration.

5.41.1 Description of the calibration procedures for the Precise Treatment
Table™ with Remote Automatic Table Movement
Calibration of the treatment table is different for systems with or without Remote Automatic
Table Movement (RATM). The calibration procedures in this chapter are applicable for Integrity™
TCS, with RATM.
Note: Remote Automatic Table Movement (RATM) is mandatory for all Integrity™ R4.0 systems.
These procedures show the movements check for the longitudinal, lateral, height, and iso-
rotation of the treatment table.

• How to do an analysis of the treatment table movement servos
• Corrective action for problems identified during service graph analysis

5.41.2 Preparing the treatment table for setting to work, test, and
calibration
If the 10-turn potentiometers are disengaged during set up, you must calibrate them. If you do
not disengage the 10-turn potentiometers, do not calibrate them.
CAUTION 5.23
Do not disengage the drive potentiometers during assembly of the treatment table. The 10-
turn coarse and check potentiometers are set up correctly in the related drive. If you ignore
this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
1 Rotate the gantry to 0°.
2 Rotate the BLD to 0°.
3 Move the treatment table until the floor scale is at zero.
4 Make sure that the:
a Column rotation axis is at 0° indent.
b Lateral and longitudinal axes are at their mechanical center position.
5 Log on to Service mode, and select the Calibration service page.
6 If necessary, do the steps that follow.
a Set the movement axis to the center position.
b Set the Coarse and Check potentiometer voltages to 5 V ±10 mV.
5.41.3 Disabling the treatment table interlock
The treatment table interlock must be disabled before you start the movement servo checks of
the iso-rotation, longitudinal, lateral, and height movement systems of the treatment table.
1 Log on to Service Mode.
2 Click the Service Functions button.
3 Click Override Machine Item.
The Override Machine Item dialog box appears.
4 In the Interlock Group, click 3 - No Term. Movm.
The machine status indicator No Term. Movm appears in the Interlock Group with an
orange background.
5 Close the Override Machine Item dialog box.

5.41.4.3 Description of the service graph content
This description is applicable for the longitudinal, lateral, height, and iso-rotation servo checks.
Use this description of the service graph content to do an analysis of the movement servos of the
treatment table.
The Figure 5.235 shows a service graph with two positive slopes, and two positive sawtooth lines.
The slopes (green (1), and red (2)) have a small gradient and tightly overlay each other. The
sawtooth lines (yellow (3), and blue (4)) are 180° out of phase with each other and fall off
suddenly.

This procedure is applicable for Integrity™ TCS, with triple potentiometers and remote automatic
table movement. This procedure gives the instructions of how to do a check of the position of the
three potentiometer systems (coarse, check, fine) in the height movement servo. And, if it is

Software items
i125 Hgt. i159 Hgt. ctrl i551 Hgt. speed
1 Rotate the treatment table through 90° on the C (column) axis.
2 Make sure that you rotate the treatment table through 90° on the C (column) axis. This will
prevent a collision between the treatment table at its highest position and the beam limiting
device (BLD) .
3 On the user interface module, operate the HEIGHT thumbwheel to lift the treatment table
to its highest position. The treatment table will stop when it is at the set limit specified in
the firmware of the EIM cabinet.

limit switches.

treatment table will stop when it is at the set limit specified in the firmware of the EIM
cabinet.
CAUTION 5.24
Do not adjust the end stop limit switches, they are set in the factory to prevent damage to the
hardware. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
Note: If the treatment table hits the end stop limit switches, use the crank handle to lower or lift the
treatment table away from the end stop limit switches. See the Precise Treatment Table™
Corrective Maintenance Manual.

5.41.12 Fine-tuning the treatment table movement
5.41.12.1 Description of fine-tuning the movement servos of the treatment table
This is a description of the the sequence to fine-tune the movement servos of the treatment
table. These procedures are applicable for Integrity™ TCS, with triple potentiometers.
The procedures to fine-tune the movement servos of the treatment table are applicable for all
the movement systems, which are the:
• Longitudinal
• Lateral
• Height
• Iso-rotation.
Fine-tune the movement servos of all the movement systems from the control room. Use the
CCTV cameras in the treatment room to see the effect of the adjustments on the treatment
table. Remove all the unwanted equipment near the treatment table before you do the
procedures.
The procedures tune the parameters of each movement servo in the sequence that follows:
• The slow down point and position gain parameters
• The threshold parameter
• The acceleration limit parameter
• The ASU increment and deadband parameters.
Related Links:
Fine-tuning the slow-down point and position gain parameters of a movement servo on page
406

To prevent the violation of IEC requirements, do not set the

141 Position gain Part 141 sets the gain of the movement servo, and therefore it

of Part 141 is too high, the treatment table can oscillate about
the set position. If the value of Part 141 is too low, then the
treatment table will not go to the set position.
If the movement oscillates or goes through the set position, you
can decrease the value of Part 141. If the treatment table does
not go to the set position, you can increase the value of Part

132 and Slow-down point and The accuracy of the treatment table movement can be different
141 position gain in the two directions (positive or negative). It is possible that the
treatment table goes farther than the set position in one
direction, but does not go to the set position in the other

get the best results in each direction.
Part 132 and Part 141 prevents the oscillation, but have
different effects. Part 132 has the most effect near the set

decreases the speed for larger errors. The Table Calibration tool
disables the Table ASU incremental gain (Part 142) while you
find the best values for Part 132 and Part 141.
135 Threshold Part 135 applies an offset to the treatment table movement.
Therefore, the ASU correction does not start from zero. This
offset decreases the time to get the ASU correction that causes
movement. Part 135 has a range of zero to 50.

142 Table ASU For small movements, it is possible that the position correction
incremental gain does not give sufficient drive to stop the friction on the motorl.
If the software senses no movement, it applies a Table ASU
correction. Part 142 increases the Table ASU correction at each
cycle (120 ms intervals) of the control system software. These
increases continue until movement occurs. When movement
occurs, the software sets the Table ASU correction to 0 again.
The Table ASU correction applies a series of small movements
(nudges) that move the treatment table to the set position. The
size of the Table ASU correction decreases as the treatment
table moves nearer to that position. Therefore, the amplitude of
the Table ASU correction that is necessary to move the
treatment table will increase. The Table ASU correction will
make a sawtooth plot in which the amplitude increases. Part
142 has a range of 0 to 5.
140 Deadband The software does not apply Table ASU correction unless the
treatment table position error is more than the value of Part
140. This stops small oscillations about the set position, but
decreases the accuracy of the treatment table position. If you
make a plot of the Table ASU correction against time, and at the
same time press the Table ASU buttons for approximately 10 to
15 seconds:
• The sawtooth plot will have one to five peaks
• The amplitude of each peak increases.
Part 140 has a range of 0 to 10, where each unit is 0.2 mm (or
0.2°).

5.41.12.8 Fine-tuning the ASU increment
This is the procedure to examine the service graph and fine-tune the ASU increment on a
movement servo on the treatment table. This is the applicable procedure for Integrity™ TCS with
triple potentiometers.
1 Examine the service graph. (This is for Part 137 only).
Note: Part 142 (which is usually set to 1) has an effect on the slope of the saw-tooth plot. If the value of
Part 142 increases, the slope of the sawtooth line increases, and the time between nudges
decreases. A value of 0 disables the nudge function.
2 Set Part 142 to the lowest value (usually 2). This will let the treatment table move to the
necessary position in 6 to 8 seconds.
3 If the treatment table is not in the necessary position in 6 to 8 seconds, you must make an
adjustment:
a To decrease the time for the treatment table to move to the necessary position,
increase the value of Part 142. It has a maximum value of 5.
b If the value of Part 142 is too high, it is possible that the treatment table will oscillate or
go through the set position (the correction intermittently changes from positive to
negative). If this occurs, decrease the value of Part 142 by 1 or 2 from its current
setting.
4 Continue to adjust the ASU increment and deadband until the ASU is stable, with no
oscillation and in a time between 6 and 8 seconds.

5.41.12.9 Fine-tuning the deadband
This is the procedure to examine the service graph and fine-tune the deadband on a movement
servo on the treatment table. This is the applicable procedure for Integrity™ TCS with triple
potentiometers.
1 Examine the service graph. Make sure that the graph shows that the treatment table
movement moves into tolerance of the set position in 10 to 15 seconds with minimum
oscillation.
Note: If the value of Part 140 is more than 5, you will not get an accuracy of 1 mm. (For iso-rotation,
this value is 1°). A large value for Part 140 will disable the nudge function. A typical value is 2.
2 If the treatment table is not in the set position in 6 to 8 seconds with minimum oscillation,
increase the value of Part 140.
3 Continue to adjust the ASU increment and deadband until the ASU is stable, with no
oscillation and in a time between 6 and 8 seconds.

5.42 Doing the treatment table post-calibration tests
This procedure is applicable for TCS with triple potentiometers, remote automatic table
movement and XVI.
1 Examine the image acquisition function.
a Make sure that XVI can acquire a VolumeView™ scan and reconstruct it without errors
and shows no messages.
b Make sure that XVI can send the corrections for the treatment table position to the
TCS.
c Use the FKP and make sure that you can move the treatment table to the set position.
2 Examine the treatment table movements.
a Use the FKP to move the treatment table through its full range of movement.
b Make sure that the treatment table operates smoothly through its full range of
movement.
c Use the HHC and make sure that Table ASU operates correctly.
3 Enable the movement system interlock.
a Make sure that all the installation tests completed satisfactorily.
b Enable the movement system interlock.
4 Make sure that the treatment table limits are set to the values in Table 1 (triple
potentiometer only).
Note: The treatment table limits are automatically set in the TCS.

6.3.3 Elements of a signal name
Signal names have three elements:
• The first three letters of the signal name identify the item being driven by the signal.
• The next letter identifies the movement axis.
• The next three letters identify the source of the signal.
A line (bar) immediately above the name of a digital signal is an indication that the signal is
active-low (logic 0). For example:
MBKXYLTU
The names of digital signals that do not have a line (bar) immediately above the name are active-
high (logic 1).

For example, signal CRSXLTU has these elements:
• CRS is a coarse potentiometer
• X is the longitudinal axis
• LTU is the level translator unit PCB.
The signal is therefore the output voltage from the level translator unit on the wiper of the
coarse potentiometer of the longitudinal movement systemt.

• The Con column gives the connector ID.

• The Level column gives the voltage or current range of the respective signal or signal

6.3.22 LCU PCB LED bar graph driver signals
The signal lines for the LED bar graph displays are internal to the LCU PCB. The signals have a 10-
bit bus and five discrete clock signals that the FPLA sends to the LED display drivers. They are
internal signals and therefore no connector, pin, and location information is necessary.

List of Warnings and Cautions
List of Warnings and Cautions
WARNING 1.1 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .7
A warning is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions, where the result can be fatal
or serious injury, injury, or clinical mistreatment, if you do not obey the instructions.
CAUTION 1.1 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .7
A caution is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions, where the result can be
damage to the equipment, data, or the environment, if you do not obey the instructions.
WARNING 1.2 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .10
Do not use this equipment unless the electrical power supply is connected to a grounded
earth to prevent an electrical shock. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.3 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .11
Do not use parts or accessories that are not approved by Elekta. The result can be incorrect
radiation delivery and other safety risks. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury
or clinical mistreatment.
WARNING 1.4 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .11
Do not lift or move heavy parts unless you obey the applicable Health and Safety regulations
and procedures. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
WARNING 1.5 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .12
Do not use a visual display unit (VDU) unless you obey the applicable Health and Safety
regulations and procedures. If you ignore this warning, it can cause injury.
WARNING 1.6 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .12
Do not use the equipment if you know or think it is defective or incorrectly calibrated. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, and damage to the
equipment.
WARNING 1.7 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .12
Do not remove, change, or override the switches, interlocks, and other safety devices. The
result can be incorrect radiation delivery and other safety risks. If you ignore this warning, it
can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
WARNING 1.8 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .12
Do not remove, change, or override the switches, interlocks, or other safety devices unless
special instructions in this document tell you to. The result can be incorrect radiation delivery
and other safety risks. When the work is completed, set all the safety devices again and do a
test of them. If you ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
WARNING 1.9 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .13
Do not start to do work on the linear accelerator before you isolate the power to the work
area at the interface cabinet. You can get a high voltage electric shock. If you ignore this
warning, it can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.10 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .13
Do not remove the covers or cables from the equipment unless special instructions in this
document tell you to. The covers give access to high voltage electric parts. Always install the
covers and cables again before you use the equipment. If you ignore this warning, it can cause
fatal injury.
Precise Treatment Table™ Document ID: 45133701862 10
Corrective Maintenance Manual
©2019 Elekta Limited. Page 611

List of Warnings and Cautions
WARNING 1.11 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .13
Do not remove the covers from the equipment unless special instructions in this document
tell you to. The covers give access to parts that move. Always install the covers again before
you use the equipment. If you ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.12 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .13
Be careful when the power supply switch of the equipment is put in the off position. There
continues to be a risk of high voltage electric shock. Some of the customer interfaces can
continue to supply electrical power to the electrical terminals. If you ignore this warning, it
can cause fatal injury.
CAUTION 1.2 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .13
Do not touch the PCBs and other electronic parts unless you wear a grounded antistatic wrist
strap. The result can be an electrostatic discharge and a malfunction. If you ignore this
caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 1.3 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .13
Do not touch the parts identified with the ESD caution label. Always obey the ESD safety
precautions when you do work on these parts. If you ignore this caution, you can cause
damage to the equipment.
WARNING 1.13 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .14
Do not use the equipment in rooms that contain explosive or flammable gases. High levels of
oxygen, and some anesthetic gases and disinfectant sprays, are explosive or flammable. If you
ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
WARNING 1.14 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .14
Do not put portable or mobile radio frequency (RF) communication devices, or other electrical
devices, near the equipment. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical
mistreatment, and damage to the equipment.
WARNING 1.15 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .14
Do not use the accessories, transducers, and cables unless they are specified by Elekta. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury and clinical mistreatment.
WARNING 1.16 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .14
Do not clean the equipment, or use a disinfectant spray, before you isolate the equipment
from the electrical supply. You can get a high voltage electric shock. If you ignore this
warning, it can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.17 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .14
Do not use a spray that is explosive or flammable. The fumes from such sprays can cause an
explosion or start a fire. If you ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury and damage to the
equipment.
CAUTION 1.4 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .14
Do not let water and other liquids go into the equipment. The results can be electrical short
circuits and metal corrosion. If you ignore this caution, it can cause damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 1.5 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .15
Do not use a spray to clean the medical equipment room. The spray can go into the
equipment. The results can be electrical short circuits and metal corrosion. If you ignore this
caution, it can cause damage to the equipment.

List of Warnings and Cautions
WARNING 1.18 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .15
Do not move or discard radioactive and hazardous material unless you are a qualified person
and have an applicable license. Obey the laws and regulations of the jurisdiction for disposal
and movement of these materials. If you ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury and
damage to the environment.
WARNING 2.1 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .19
Do not operate the equipment unless you correctly complete the safety checks, planned
maintenance schedules, and extended life checks. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
WARNING 3.1 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .115
Do not change the calibration of the gantry or treatment table movement, unless you copy
the applicable changed calibration data to the XVI software. Use the XVI DCB utility in the XVI
software to copy the changed calibration data. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
clinical mistreatment.
WARNING 4.1 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .120
Do not lift or move heavy parts unless you obey the applicable Health and Safety regulations
and procedures. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
WARNING 4.2 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .123
Do not lift or move heavy parts unless you obey the applicable Health and Safety regulations
and procedures. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
WARNING 4.3 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .128
Do not use fasteners other than the four high tensile bolts supplied to install the safety
support strut. If you use other fasteners, the treatment table can fall. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause serious injury and damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 4.1 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .129
Do not move the treatment table when the safety support strut is installed. If you ignore this
caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
WARNING 4.4 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .130
Do not use motorized movement of the treatment table until after you remove the manual
crank handle. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
CAUTION 5.1 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .170
Do not let the floor pan tilt when you lift it. If the floor pan tilts, it can catch on the brake,
which will prevent free movement. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the
equipment.
WARNING 5.1 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .173
Do not lift or move heavy parts unless you obey the applicable Health and Safety regulations
and procedures. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
CAUTION 5.2 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .173
Do not let the floor pan tilt when you lift it. If the floor pan tilts, it can catch on the brake,
which will prevent free movement. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the
equipment.
CAUTION 5.3 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .173
Do not install the floor pan with the iso-rotation brake engaged. Release the iso-rotation
brake before you install the floor pan. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the
brake pads.

List of Warnings and Cautions
CAUTION 5.4 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .182
Do not fully tighten all the fasteners before all covers are installed. If you ignore this caution,
you can cause damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 5.5 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .204
Do not touch the printed circuit boards (PCBs) and other electronic parts unless you wear a
grounded antistatic wrist strap. If you do not wear a wrist strap you can get an electrostatic
discharge, and this can also have an effect of the operation of the PCB. If you ignore this
caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 5.6 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .206
Do not touch the PCBs and other electronic parts unless you wear a grounded antistatic wrist
strap. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 5.7 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .207
Do not rotate the column with the bellows covers tilted as this can cause damage to the
casting and the bellows cover. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the
equipment.
CAUTION 5.8 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .225
Do not move the lateral or longitudinal carriage assembly more than its usual range of
movement because some of the roller bearings can disengage from the bearing channels. If
you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
WARNING 5.2 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .254
Do not put your fingers in the drive track mechanism after the finger trap cover plates are
removed. Install the finger trap cover plates immediately after the work is completed. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause injury.
CAUTION 5.9 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .256
Do not move the lateral or longitudinal carriage assembly more than its usual range of
movement because some of the roller bearings can disengage from the bearing channels. If
you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
WARNING 5.3 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .267
